topics.title: Consumer Behaviour in the Automotive Sector - Understanding Consumer Preferences, Decision Making Processes and Buying Behaviour Specific to the Automotive Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Consumer Behaviour in the Automotive Sector - Understanding Consumer Preferences, Decision Making Processes and Buying Behaviour Specific to the Automotive Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Consumer Behaviour** is formally defined as the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. In the context of the **automotive sector**, consumer behaviour encompasses the entire psychological, social, cultural, and economic process through which a potential buyer identifies a need for a vehicle, searches for information, evaluates alternatives, makes a purchase decision, and engages in post-purchase behaviour.

According to the **KTU 2024 Marketing Management syllabus**, consumer behaviour analysis is the foundational step in marketing strategy formulation, particularly in high-involvement product categories like automobiles, where:

- **High-Involvement Products**: Products that carry significant financial, emotional, and social risk to the buyer.
- **Decision-Making Unit (DMU)**: The group of individuals (initiator, influencer, decider, buyer, user) involved in the automotive purchase process.
- **Evoked Set**: The limited set of brands/models a consumer seriously considers during evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The automotive industry is the canonical example used in KTU 2024 Scheme to illustrate *complex consumer behaviour* because it combines rational evaluation (price, fuel efficiency, safety ratings) with emotional drivers (status, brand identity, lifestyle aspiration). Both dimensions must be analysed separately and then integrated for a complete marketing understanding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to buy a smartphone. You don't just walk into a shop and grab the first one you see. Instead, you:

1. **Realize** your old phone is slow (problem recognition).
2. **Ask friends** and read online reviews (information search).
3. **Compare** Samsung vs. Apple vs. OnePlus on specs and price (evaluation of alternatives).
4. **Choose** one based on your budget and brand affinity (purchase decision).
5. **Feel happy or regretful** after using it for a month (post-purchase behaviour).

**Now scale this exact same process up by a factor of 10x — and you have automotive consumer behaviour.** The only differences are:

- The financial commitment is **years of EMI payments** (typically 5–7 years), not a one-time swipe.
- The **brand symbolism** is far more visible — your car is a public statement of identity, status, and lifestyle.
- The **decision timeline** stretches from weeks to months (or even years for first-time buyers).
- The **involvement of family members** (spouse, parents, peers) is much higher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** Consumer behaviour in the automotive sector is essentially the "story" of how and why people choose, use, and experience cars — and understanding this story is what allows companies like Maruti, Hyundai, Tata, Toyota, and BMW to position their vehicles effectively in the Indian market.

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Terms

| Term | Definition | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Need** | A basic biological/motivational state | Need for transportation |
| **Want** | A need shaped by culture and personality | Want a SUV with sunroof |
| **Demand** | A want backed by purchasing power | Demand for SUVs under ₹15 Lakhs |
| **Customer Value** | Benefits received minus costs incurred | Mileage + Status − Purchase Price |
| **Customer Satisfaction** | Feeling of pleasure/displeasure from performance vs. expectations | Happy with fuel economy post-purchase |
| **Brand Loyalty** | Consistent preference for one brand over time | Buying a third Honda City in a row |
| **Involvement** | Level of perceived personal importance of the purchase | High for cars, low for salt |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Consumer Decision-Making Process (Five-Stage Model)

The **Engel-Blackwell-Miniard (EBM) Model** is the canonical framework adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus. It describes consumer decision-making as a five-stage sequential process, with two additional stages (outcome stages) sometimes appended for high-involvement products like automobiles.

#### Stage 1 — Need Recognition (Problem Recognition)

This is the trigger event where the consumer perceives a gap between their **current state** and their **desired state**.

- **Automotive Triggers**: Old car breakdown, family expansion (need for 7-seater), salary increment, social comparison (neighbour bought a new car), change in job location.
- **Marketing Implication**: Marketers use advertising, seasonal offers, and lifestyle campaigns to activate latent needs.

#### Stage 2 — Information Search

Once a need is recognized, the consumer begins searching for information. There are two levels:

- **Internal Search**: Recalling past experiences with cars, brand memories, personal preferences.
- **External Search**:
  - **Personal sources**: Family, friends, colleagues, online forums (Team-BHP, CarWale, AutoCar India).
  - **Commercial sources**: Dealership visits, advertisements, sales personnel, test drives.
  - **Public sources**: Consumer reports, J.D. Power ratings, NCAP safety ratings, government websites.
  - **Experiential sources**: Test drives, showroom handling, inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about automotive consumer behaviour, always mention the **online-offline information search blend** — modern automotive consumers typically do 60–70% of their research online before visiting a single dealership (this is called the "Digital Decision Journey").

#### Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives

The consumer compares the various brands/models identified during the search phase using evaluative criteria. For automobiles, the criteria are often weighted differently by different consumer segments:

| Criterion | Weightage (First-time buyer) | Weightage (Upgrade buyer) | Weightage (Luxury buyer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price / EMI affordability** | **High (40%)** | Medium (20%) | Low (10%) |
| **Fuel Efficiency** | High (25%) | Medium (15%) | Low (5%) |
| **Brand Reputation** | Medium (10%) | High (20%) | High (25%) |
| **Features & Technology** | Medium (10%) | High (20%) | High (25%) |
| **Safety Rating (NCAP)** | Medium (10%) | High (15%) | High (15%) |
| **Resale Value** | Low (5%) | High (10%) | Medium (10%) |
| **Status / Aesthetics** | Low (0%) | Medium (0%) | High (10%) |

#### Stage 4 — Purchase Decision

The culmination of the previous stages, where the consumer actually buys a vehicle. However, two factors can intervene between evaluation and purchase:

- **Attitudes of others** (negative feedback from a family member).
- **Situational factors** (unexpected financial emergency, dealer stock unavailability, better competitor offer).

In the **Indian automotive context**, the actual purchase is often influenced by:

- **Festive season offers** (Diwali, Onam, Dhanteras).
- **Exchange bonus** on old vehicle.
- **Corporate fleet discounts**.
- **Loan pre-approval** status.
- **Dealership relationship and trust**.

#### Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour

The stage that determines whether the customer becomes a **brand advocate** or a **brand critic**. Cognitive Dissonance (post-purchase anxiety) is particularly strong in high-involvement purchases like cars.

- **Post-Purchase Satisfaction** → Word-of-mouth referrals, repeat purchase, brand loyalty.
- **Post-Purchase Dissatisfaction** → Negative reviews, complaints to dealer, brand switching, social media criticism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Extension — Stages 6 & 7 (Disposal & Re-purchase):** In automotive markets, the post-purchase stage now extends to **vehicle disposal** (resale, scrappage) and **re-purchase intention**, which are heavily influenced by resale value perception, brand service experience, and new product launches.

### 2.2 Factors Influencing Automotive Consumer Behaviour

The classic KTU framework divides influencing factors into **four major categories**:

#### A. Cultural Factors (Broadest, most fundamental)

- **Culture**: The set of basic values, perceptions, wants, and behaviours learned from family and institutions. In India, *family orientation* and *status consciousness* heavily influence car choice (e.g., preference for sedans over hatchbacks in certain regions).
- **Subculture**: Smaller cultural groups based on religion, region (South Indian preferences differ from North Indian), caste, or age cohort. SUV craze in Punjab vs. compact car preference in Kerala.
- **Social Class**: Reflected in occupation, income, education. Determines whether one shops in the mass market (Maruti, Hyundai) or premium market (Honda, Toyota, VW) or luxury market (BMW, Mercedes, Audi).

#### B. Social Factors

- **Reference Groups**: Family (most influential in India), friends, work colleagues, online communities (Team-BHP, automotive YouTube channels).
- **Family**: The husband-wife-parent dynamic is critical. Often, the wife influences aesthetic and safety choices, the husband focuses on performance, and parents prioritize value and reliability.
- **Roles and Status**: A senior executive may buy a Mercedes to reflect their organizational position.

#### C. Personal Factors

- **Age and Life-Cycle Stage**: A 25-year-old first jobber buys a hatchback; a 40-year-old family man buys an SUV.
- **Occupation**: Doctor prefers Skoda Octavia; Tech CEO prefers Tesla or BMW.
- **Economic Situation**: Disposable income, savings, credit availability.
- **Lifestyle**: Active outdoor lifestyle → Thar/Scorpio; Urban commuter → Swift/Baleno.
- **Personality and Self-Concept**: Self-concept (how one sees oneself) and ideal self-concept (how one wishes to be seen) drive aspirational purchases.

#### D. Psychological Factors

- **Motivation**: Maslow's Hierarchy applied — physiological (basic transport), safety (NCAP rating, airbags), esteem (luxury brands), self-actualization (custom-modified vehicles).
- **Perception**: How a consumer selectively perceives, organizes, and interprets information. A car ad emphasising "youth" appeals to a different perception than one emphasising "family safety."
- **Learning**: Repeated exposure to a brand, test drive experiences, and word-of-mouth create learned preferences.
- **Beliefs and Attitudes**: Pre-existing beliefs ("Tata is safe," "Japanese cars are reliable") strongly bias automotive choices in India.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas & Frameworks for Consumer Behaviour Analysis**

| Framework / Formula | Expression / Description | Application to Automotive Sector |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Perceived Value (CPV)** | $\text{CPV} = \text{Total Benefits} - \text{Total Costs}$ | Benefits: features, status, fuel savings. Costs: price, EMI, insurance, maintenance, time. |
| **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** | $\text{CSAT} = \text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectations}$ | If performance ≥ expectations → satisfied customer → repeat purchase and referrals. |
| **AIDA Model** | Attention → Interest → Desire → Action | Used in automotive advertising to guide customer through the funnel. |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Auto brands measure NPS post-delivery to gauge word-of-mouth potential. |
| **Brand Equity (Aaker Model)** | $BE = f(\text{Awareness}, \text{Loyalty}, \text{Quality}, \text{Associations})$ | Maruti has high brand equity due to perceived quality and service network. |
| **Evoked Set Size** | $E = \{B_1, B_2, ..., B_n\}$ where $n \le 5$ typically | Most car buyers have an evoked set of 3–5 models before final choice. |
| **Involvement Level** | $I = f(\text{Cost}, \text{Risk}, \text{Identity Expression})$ | Cars score very high on all three dimensions. |
| **Reference Group Influence (Park & Lessig)** | $I_{RG} = f(\text{Conformity}, \text{Information}, \text{Utilitarian}, \text{Value Expressive})$ | Friends' opinions on car choice = informational + value-expressive influence. |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(\text{Revenue}_t - \text{Cost}_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$ | Used by auto companies to justify long-term service and loyalty investments. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marketing

Understanding automotive consumer behaviour is the foundation for:

- **Segmentation**: Identifying groups like "Young Urban Professionals" or "Family-oriented SUV Buyers."
- **Targeting**: Selecting which segment to focus marketing budget on.
- **Positioning**: Crafting a unique image (e.g., Volvo = Safety, BMW = Ultimate Driving, Tata = Affordable Innovation).
- **Product Design**: Feature prioritization based on what consumers value.
- **Pricing Strategy**: EMI structuring, exchange bonuses, dynamic pricing.
- **Channel Strategy**: Online booking vs. physical dealership.
- **Promotion Strategy**: Digital ads for young buyers, TV/print for older buyers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Application of the Consumer Decision-Making Process in the Automotive Sector

To make the theory operationally clear, let us trace the journey of a typical **first-time Indian car buyer** through the complete decision-making process.

> **Case Profile:**
> - **Name (hypothetical):** Mr. Arjun Menon
> - **Age:** 28 years
> - **Occupation:** Software Engineer, Bengaluru
> - **Annual Income:** ₹12 Lakhs
> - **Current State:** Owns a 2015 Honda Activa scooter
> - **Desired State:** Personal four-wheeler for family (wife, expected first child) and weekend trips

#### Step 1 — Need Recognition (Problem Recognition)

The consumer perceives a gap:

$$\text{Need Gap} = \text{Desired State} - \text{Current State}$$

For Arjun:

$$\text{Need Gap} = \{\text{Four-wheeler, safety, comfort, family use}\} - \{\text{Two-wheeler, weather exposure, limited safety}\}$$

**Trigger Events for Arjun:**

1. Wife's pregnancy announcement (need for safer transport).
2. Rainy season commute difficulty.
3. Colleague bought a new Hyundai Creta (social comparison).
4. Company announced 15% salary increment (increased purchasing power).

> **Marketing Implication:** Car companies time campaigns around monsoon, festive seasons, salary cycles, and pregnancy announcements to activate need recognition.

#### Step 2 — Information Search

Arjun engages in **both internal and external search**:

**Internal Search (Memory-based):**
- Recalls childhood family car (Maruti Alto).
- Remembers friend's Tata Nexon experience.
- Personal preference: Likes blue colour, automatic transmission.

**External Search (Active research):**

| Source Type | Specific Examples Used by Arjun |
|---|---|
| **Personal** | Father (recommends Maruti), Wife (wants sunroof), Friends (split opinion on Hyundai vs. Kia) |
| **Commercial** | Hyundai website, Kia ads on YouTube, Maruti dealer visit |
| **Public** | Global NCAP ratings, CarWale reviews, Autocar India YouTube channel |
| **Experiential** | Test drives of Creta, Seltos, and Grand Vitara |

**Information Search Duration:** Approximately 2–3 months for a first-time car buyer in India.

#### Step 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives

Arjun's **Evoked Set** narrows to 3 models:

$$\text{Evoked Set (Arjun)} = \{\text{Hyundai Creta}, \text{Kia Seltos}, \text{Maruti Grand Vitara}\}$$

**Multi-Attribute Model — Weighted Evaluation:**

Each model is evaluated on attributes weighted by personal importance.

Let weights be $w_i$ and brand scores be $s_{ij}$ (on a 1–10 scale).

**Total Score for Brand j:**

$$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}$$

**Detailed Calculation Table:**

| Attribute | Weight $w_i$ | Hyundai Creta $s_{i1}$ | Kia Seltos $s_{i2}$ | Maruti Grand Vitara $s_{i3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Price / Value for Money | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | **9** |
| Fuel Efficiency | 0.20 | 8 | 7 | **9** |
| Safety (NCAP Rating) | 0.20 | **9** (6-star GNCAP) | 8 | 7 |
| Features & Tech | 0.15 | **9** | 8 | 7 |
| Brand Trust & Service | 0.10 | **9** | 7 | **10** |
| Aesthetics / Status | 0.10 | 8 | **9** | 7 |

**Now calculating Total Scores:**

**For Hyundai Creta:**

$$S_{Creta} = (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8)$$

$$S_{Creta} = 1.75 + 1.60 + 1.80 + 1.35 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 8.20$$

**For Kia Seltos:**

$$S_{Seltos} = (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9)$$

$$S_{Seltos} = 2.00 + 1.40 + 1.60 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.90 = 7.80$$

**For Maruti Grand Vitara:**

$$S_{GrandVitara} = (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 10) + (0.10 \times 7)$$

$$S_{GrandVitara} = 2.25 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 1.05 + 1.00 + 0.70 = 8.20$$

**Result:** Hyundai Creta and Maruti Grand Vitara tie at 8.20. Arjun must then apply **non-compensatory decision rules** (e.g., budget cut-off) to break the tie.

**Non-Compensatory Rule Applied:** "EMI must not exceed ₹25,000/month."

- Grand Vitara EMI at 0% down, 7-year tenure ≈ ₹24,500/month ✓
- Creta EMI at 0% down, 7-year tenure ≈ ₹26,500/month ✗

**Decision:** Grand Vitara qualifies. Final choice → **Maruti Grand Vitara**.

#### Step 4 — Purchase Decision

Arjun's purchase is finalized, but two **intervening factors** could derail it:

1. **Negative word-of-mouth**: Father-in-law expresses concern about Maruti's build quality → may cause re-evaluation.
2. **Situational factor**: Dealer runs out of the preferred blue colour → may delay purchase or trigger brand switch.

**Mitigations:** Arjun visits the dealer with his father-in-law for a test drive; dealer offers a custom-order timeline of 3 weeks.

**Final Action:** Arjun signs the purchase order, pays the booking amount, arranges finance through SBI Car Loan (8.5% interest rate), and schedules delivery during Dhanteras for a free accessory kit.

#### Step 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour

After delivery, two scenarios are possible:

**Scenario A — Post-Purchase Satisfaction:**

$$\text{Satisfaction} = f(\text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectations})$$

If Grand Vitara's real-world mileage (18 kmpl) meets or exceeds Arjun's expectation (17 kmpl) → **Satisfaction** → Positive WOM, high CLV, brand loyalty.

**Scenario B — Cognitive Dissonance (Post-Purchase Anxiety):**

If performance falls short (e.g., 14 kmpl actual vs. 17 kmpl claimed) → **Dissonance** → Arjun starts reading negative reviews, regrets not choosing Creta, may sell within 2 years.

**Marketing Response:** Maruti offers proactive service follow-ups, free first three services, and a dedicated customer care line to reduce dissonance.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Consumer Behaviour Across Car Segments

| Behavioural Dimension | Entry Hatchback (e.g., Alto) | Mid-SUV (e.g., Creta) | Luxury (e.g., BMW 3 Series) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Involvement Level** | Medium-High (first major purchase) | High | Very High |
| **Decision Time** | 1–3 months | 3–6 months | 6–12 months |
| **Information Sources** | Family, friends, YouTube | Auto portals, experts, test drives | Brand website, peer network, lifestyle events |
| **Dominant Decision Criteria** | Price, mileage, low maintenance | Safety, features, brand | Status, performance, brand heritage |
| **Dominant Psychological Driver** | Achievement (owning first car) | Family security + status | Self-actualization + identity |
| **Post-Purchase Behaviour** | High service interaction | Moderate | Low frequency but high expectations |
| **Brand Loyalty Potential** | High (if first experience positive) | Medium | Very High (if emotional bond formed) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Stage Automotive Consumer Decision-Making Model

This diagram illustrates the complete journey of an automotive consumer, including the influencing factors that act on each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Need Recognition] --> B[Stage 2: Information Search]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Purchase Decision]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Post Purchase Behaviour]
    E --> F{Outcome}
    F -->|Satisfied| G[Brand Loyalty and Repeat Purchase]
    F -->|Dissatisfied| H[Brand Switching and Negative WOM]
    G --> I[Future Re-purchase Loop]
    H --> I
    I --> A

    A -. Influenced by .-> J[Cultural Factors]
    A -. Influenced by .-> K[Social Factors]
    B -. Influenced by .-> L[Personal Factors]
    C -. Influenced by .-> M[Psychological Factors]
    D -. Influenced by .-> N[Situational Factors]
```

### 4.2 Factors Influencing Automotive Consumer Behaviour — Block Diagram

This block-level architecture shows how the four major factor categories interact and collectively shape the consumer's purchase behaviour.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CulturalLayer[Cultural Factors Layer]
        C1[Culture]
        C2[Subculture]
        C3[Social Class]
    end

    subgraph SocialLayer[Social Factors Layer]
        S1[Reference Groups]
        S2[Family]
        S3[Roles and Status]
    end

    subgraph PersonalLayer[Personal Factors Layer]
        P1[Age and Life Cycle]
        P2[Occupation and Income]
        P3[Lifestyle]
        P4[Personality and Self Concept]
    end

    subgraph PsychologicalLayer[Psychological Factors Layer]
        Y1[Motivation]
        Y2[Perception]
        Y3[Learning]
        Y4[Beliefs and Attitudes]
    end

    CulturalLayer --> Consumer[Automotive Consumer Behaviour]
    SocialLayer --> Consumer
    PersonalLayer --> Consumer
    PsychologicalLayer --> Consumer

    Consumer --> Decision[Purchase Decision]
    Decision --> Action[Car Selection and Purchase]
```

### 4.3 Decision-Making Unit (DMU) in Family Car Purchase

The automotive purchase is rarely a solo decision. This diagram maps the roles played by different family members.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Husband Initiator and User] -->|Influences| D[Final Decision]
    B[Wife Influencer on Aesthetics and Safety] -->|Influences| D
    C[Parents Advisors on Value and Reliability] -->|Influences| D
    E[Children Aspirational Influencers Brand Preference] -->|Influences| D
    F[Friends Peer Group Opinion] -->|Influences| D
    G[Dealership Salesperson Information Provider] -->|Influences| D
    D[Decision Maker] --> H[Final Car Purchase]
```

### 4.4 The AIDA Model Application in Automotive Marketing

The AIDA framework guides customers from initial awareness to final action — applied specifically to a typical automotive marketing campaign.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attention: TV Ad, Social Media, Outdoor Billboard] --> B[Interest: Website Visit, Brochure Download, YouTube Reviews]
    B --> C[Desire: Test Drive, Virtual Showroom, Customer Testimonials]
    C --> D[Action: Booking, Loan Approval, Delivery]
    D --> E[Advocacy: Word of Mouth, Online Reviews, Referrals]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define consumer behaviour. How is it different in the automotive sector compared to FMCG products?

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Consumer behaviour refers to the study of how individuals or groups select, purchase, use, and dispose of products, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants (Schiffman & Kanuk, 2010).

**Difference in Automotive Sector (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Automotive Sector | FMCG Sector |
|---|---|---|
| **Involvement** | High (financial, emotional, social risk) | Low to medium |
| **Decision Time** | Weeks to months | Minutes to days |
| **Information Search** | Extensive (online + offline, family consultations) | Minimal (habitual/impulse) |
| **Decision Maker** | Multiple family members (DMU) | Often individual |
| **Post-purchase dissonance** | Very high | Low |

**Conclusion:** Automotive consumer behaviour is complex, multi-stakeholder, and high-involvement, requiring detailed marketing analysis.

---

#### Question 2: What is an "evoked set" in the context of automotive consumer decision-making?

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The **evoked set** is the limited number of brands or models (typically 3–5) that a consumer seriously considers as acceptable alternatives during the evaluation stage of the decision-making process. In the automotive sector, the evoked set is formed after extensive information search and is influenced by brand awareness, past experience, peer recommendations, and personal preferences.

**Example (1 Mark):**
For a buyer evaluating a mid-size SUV, the evoked set may include Hyundai Creta, Kia Seltos, Maruti Grand Vitara, and Toyota Urban Cruiser Hyryder. The final purchase is made from within this set.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **[Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Each Part B question has two alternatives; students answer either one]**

---

#### **Option A (14 Marks):** Analyse the five-stage consumer decision-making process with specific reference to the Indian automotive sector.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, ESE Pattern]**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Introduction to the Model (3 Marks):**

The **Engel-Blackwell-Miniard (EBM) Model** is the most widely accepted framework for understanding consumer decision-making. It describes a sequential five-stage process applicable to high-involvement products like automobiles. In the Indian context, where cars represent both functional mobility and social status, this model is particularly relevant.

**(b) Detailed Stage-wise Analysis (8 Marks — 1.5 Marks per stage + 0.5 for transition):**

**Stage 1 — Need Recognition:**
- Triggered by life events (marriage, baby, job change), economic improvement, social comparison.
- Example: A young professional in Kochi realizing the need for personal transport after the metro route to office becomes crowded.

**Stage 2 — Information Search:**
- Internal search: Memory, brand preferences.
- External search: Online (CarWale, ZigWheels, YouTube), offline (dealerships, friends, family).
- **Indian context:** 70% of research is digital before the first showroom visit.

**Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives:**
- Multi-attribute model: Consumers assign weights to features (price, mileage, safety, brand).
- Evoked set of 3–5 models is evaluated.
- Example: A buyer evaluating Swift, Baleno, and i20.

**Stage 4 — Purchase Decision:**
- Influenced by family approval, dealer trust, loan availability, festival offers.
- **Indian context:** Diwali, Dhanteras, Onam drive 30–40% of annual car sales.

**Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour:**
- Cognitive dissonance is common.
- Service experience, resale value, and brand community determine loyalty.
- Example: Satisfied Tata Nexon owners becoming brand advocates on social media.

**(c) Modern Extensions and Indian Market Specifics (3 Marks):**

- **Digital Decision Journey:** Online configuration, virtual test drives, online booking.
- **Stages 6 & 7:** Disposal (resale, scrappage policy) and re-purchase.
- **Family-centric decision-making:** The DMU is broader in India than in Western markets.
- **Aspirational vs. Need-based buying:** A large segment buys cars as a status symbol rather than pure utility.

**Conclusion:**
The five-stage model provides a robust framework for understanding Indian automotive consumer behaviour, with cultural, family, and digital factors playing dominant roles.

---

#### **Option B (14 Marks):** Discuss the various psychological and social factors that influence consumer buying behaviour in the automotive sector. Illustrate with suitable examples.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Introduction (2 Marks):**

Consumer buying behaviour in the automotive sector is shaped by a complex interplay of psychological and social factors. Unlike FMCG purchases, car buying involves high financial commitment, emotional involvement, and strong social signalling — making these factors critically important for marketers to understand.

**(b) Psychological Factors (6 Marks):**

**1. Motivation (1.5 Marks):**
- Maslow's Hierarchy applied: A first car satisfies *esteem* needs; a luxury car addresses *self-actualization*.
- Example: A newly promoted manager buying a Toyota Fortuner to reflect status.

**2. Perception (1.5 Marks):**
- Selective attention, distortion, and retention influence car advertisements' effectiveness.
- Example: A safety-conscious buyer perceives NCAP ratings as decisive; a status-conscious buyer ignores them.

**3. Learning (1.5 Marks):**
- Repeated brand exposure, positive test drive experiences, and word-of-mouth create learned preferences.
- Example: A consumer who had a good experience with Maruti service becomes a repeat buyer.

**4. Beliefs and Attitudes (1.5 Marks):**
- Pre-existing beliefs ("Japanese cars are reliable," "German cars are engineered well") strongly influence choices.
- Example: Strong belief in Toyota's reliability drives demand for Innova Hycross despite premium pricing.

**(c) Social Factors (6 Marks):**

**1. Reference Groups (1.5 Marks):**
- Family, friends, online communities (Team-BHP) act as strong influencers.
- Example: Recommendations from a car-owning friend often outweigh dealer claims.

**2. Family (1.5 Marks):**
- Husband-wife-parent triad is central in Indian car buying.
- Example: Wife may veto a sporty hatchback in favour of a family SUV.

**3. Roles and Status (1.5 Marks):**
- Occupation and social position dictate the segment one shops in.
- Example: A doctor buying a Skoda Superb to reflect professional achievement.

**4. Opinion Leaders (1.5 Marks):**
- Auto bloggers, YouTube reviewers (e.g., PowerDrift, AutoCar India) shape perception.
- Example: A glowing review by a popular YouTuber can spike showroom footfalls.

**Conclusion (mark included in part c):**
A successful automotive marketing strategy must address both rational psychological drivers and emotional social influences, recognizing that Indian consumers rarely buy cars based on a single factor.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Vague definitions (–1 mark):** Students often define consumer behaviour loosely. Always cite the buyer-decision process and high-involvement product nature for automotive.
> 2. **Ignoring DMU concept (–2 marks):** In questions on family decision-making, forgetting to mention the **Decision-Making Unit (Initiator, Influencer, Decider, Buyer, User)** is a frequent error.
> 3. **Skipping Indian context (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU examiners specifically reward **Indian examples** (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai, festivals like Diwali/Onam, NCAP ratings, EMI culture). Generic Western examples lose marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning digital shift (–1 mark):** In any 2024 KTU paper, ignoring the **Digital Decision Journey** (online research, virtual showrooms, online booking) is considered outdated.
> 5. **Confusing stages:** Students sometimes merge *Information Search* and *Evaluation of Alternatives* — these must be explained as **separate stages**.
> 6. **Listing without analysis (–2 to –3 marks):** Simply listing the four factor types (cultural, social, personal, psychological) without **explaining** how each operates in the automotive context loses substantial marks.
> 7. **Forgetting post-purchase:** Many answers end at the *Purchase Decision* — KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **post-purchase behaviour, cognitive dissonance, and brand loyalty** as integral parts of the model.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Consumer Behaviour (CB):** The study of how people select, buy, use, and dispose of products. In automotive, it is **high-involvement, complex, family-influenced**, and **digitally researched**.
- ✅ **Five-Stage Model (EBM):** Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour. Modern extension: Disposal + Re-purchase.
- ✅ **Evoked Set:** 3–5 brands/models the consumer seriously considers.
- ✅ **DMU (Decision-Making Unit):** Initiator, Influencer, Decider, Buyer, User — critical in Indian family car buying.
- ✅ **Four Factor Categories:**
  - **Cultural:** Culture, Subculture, Social Class
  - **Social:** Reference Groups, Family, Roles & Status
  - **Personal:** Age, Occupation, Lifestyle, Personality, Economic Situation
  - **Psychological:** Motivation, Perception, Learning, Beliefs & Attitudes
- ✅ **Cognitive Dissonance:** Post-purchase anxiety common in car buying — must be managed via communication and service.
- ✅ **AIDA Model:** Attention → Interest → Desire → Action — used in automotive advertising.
- ✅ **Customer Perceived Value (CPV):** Benefits minus Costs — guides purchase justification.
- ✅ **NPS (Net Promoter Score):** Used to measure post-purchase advocacy.
- ✅ **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):** Long-term value of a car buyer including service, resale, and referrals.
- ✅ **Indian Market Specifics to Always Mention:** Diwali/Onam sales, NCAP awareness, EMI culture, family decision-making, digital-first research journey, service network importance, Maruti-Tata-Hyundai dominance.
- ✅ **Luxury vs. Mass Market:** Behavioural drivers differ — luxury = status, identity, performance; mass market = price, mileage, reliability.
- ✅ **Digital Decision Journey:** 60–70% of car research happens online before dealership visit — modern marketers must integrate digital and physical touchpoints.
- ✅ **Aspirational Buying:** In India, cars are often bought as **status symbols** beyond pure utility — this is a key cultural insight.
- ✅ **Valuation Keywords to Use in KTU Answers:** *High-involvement*, *Evoked Set*, *DMU*, *Multi-attribute Model*, *Cognitive Dissonance*, *Reference Group Influence*, *AIDA*, *CLV*, *NPS*, *Digital Decision Journey*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Segmentation and Targeting - Techniques for Segmenting Automotive Markets Based on Demographic, Psychographic and Behavioural Factors, Strategies for Targeting Specific Customer Segments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Market Segmentation and Targeting - Techniques for Segmenting Automotive Markets Based on Demographic, Psychographic and Behavioural Factors, Strategies for Targeting Specific Customer Segments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Market Segmentation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Market segmentation* is the strategic process of **dividing a broad heterogeneous market** of potential car buyers into **smaller, homogeneous sub-groups** (segments) based on shared characteristics such as demographics, psychographics, behaviour, or geography. Within each segment, buyers exhibit **similar needs, wants, and responses** to marketing programmes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Market Targeting (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Market targeting* (or *targeting*) is the subsequent act of **evaluating each market segment's attractiveness** and then **selecting one or more segments** to enter with a customised marketing mix (Product, Price, Place, Promotion — the 4Ps).

In KTU board terminology, segmentation and targeting are the first two stages of the classical **STP Framework** (Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning), which sits at the heart of the Marketing Management syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board-Exam Point:**
> Segmentation and Targeting are *analytical and strategic* marketing decisions made **before** designing the marketing mix. They determine *whom* the firm serves, not *how*.

## 1.2 The STP Funnel — A Bird's-Eye View

Think of the STP funnel as a three-stage filter:

$$
\text{Heterogeneous Market} \xrightarrow{\text{1. Segment}} \text{Homogeneous Sub-groups} \xrightarrow{\text{2. Target}} \text{Chosen Segment(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{3. Position}} \text{Brand Perception}
$$

The automobile industry is a textbook case of segmentation because cars are **high-involvement, high-priced, durable goods** that customers purchase infrequently (every 5–10 years) and on the basis of complex, multi-attribute decisions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bus Stop" Intuition

Imagine you are the marketing head of a bus company in a city. The city has **10 lakh people**, all needing transport, but they are clearly different:
- Office-goers want **AC comfort and punctuality** (demographic + behavioural).
- College students want **low-cost, high-frequency** buses (income + usage rate).
- Senior citizens want **gentle boarding, low-floor buses** (psychographic — values accessibility).

You do **not** run the same bus for everyone. You design *different bus services* for *different groups*. That is segmentation + targeting. The same logic — only far more sophisticated — applies to cars, SUVs, two-wheelers, and EVs.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorable KTU Mnemonic for STP:**
> **S** — *Slice* the market
> **T** — *Take* the best slice
> **P** — *Place* your brand uniquely in that slice

## 1.4 Why the Automotive Industry Demands Segmentation

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Heterogeneous demand** | From ₹2 lakh two-wheelers to ₹2 crore super-cars — one product cannot serve all. |
| **High customer involvement** | Purchase is infrequent and considered; buyers actively compare. |
| **Long product life-cycles** | 5–7 year model cycles demand long-term segment focus. |
| **Diverse usage occasions** | Family trips, off-roading, commuting, status display, fleet use. |
| **Globalisation** | OEMs like Maruti, Hyundai, Tata operate in 50+ segments simultaneously. |
| **Electric transition** | New EV segments (early adopters, eco-warriors, tech-enthusiasts) emerge. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Criteria for Effective Market Segmentation (Kotler's 5 Tests)

For a segment to be useful in KTU board answers, it must clear all five tests. Memorise the acronym **MADS-A**:

| Test | Meaning | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **M**easurable | Size, profile, purchasing power can be quantified. | "Young urban professionals aged 25–35, income ₹8–15 lakh" — measurable. |
| **A**ccessible | Reachable through existing distribution/media. | Reach via Instagram, auto-expos, metro-city dealers. |
| **D**ifferentiable | Conceptually distinguishable from other segments. | "Luxury SUV buyers" ≠ "Family sedan buyers." |
| **S**ubstantial (Profitable) | Large enough to be worth serving. | Tier-1 cities alone have ~6 lakh premium-car buyers/year. |
| **A**ctionable | Effective programmes can be designed to attract & serve. | Tata can design a clear Nexon EV campaign for eco-conscious millennials. |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error in KTU exams: defining only 3–4 criteria. **All five** must appear in a 14-mark answer.

## 2.2 The Four Major Bases of Segmentation (Automotive Context)

### 2.2.1 Demographic Segmentation

The most widely used base. Splits market by *objective, statistical* variables.

| Variable | Sub-classification | Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Age** | Gen-Z (≤25), Millennials (26–40), Gen-X (41–55), Boomers (56+) | Gen-Z → entry hatchbacks (Maruti Alto, Swift); Boomers → premium sedans (Camry, Accord). |
| **Gender** | Male, Female, Other | Women-targeted compact SUVs (Tata Punch *Pride* edition); safety-led marketing. |
| **Income** | Lower (≤5L), Middle (5–25L), Upper-Middle (25L–1Cr), Luxury (>1Cr) | Maruti 800 → Mercedes S-Class ladder. |
| **Education** | School, Graduate, Post-graduate | Tech-loaded EVs target post-grads; basic commuters targeted at all. |
| **Family Size / Life-cycle** | Bachelor → Newly married → Full-nest → Empty-nest → Solitary survivor | Full-nest families → 7-seater SUVs (Innova, Fortuner, Safari). |
| **Occupation** | Student, salaried, professional, business-owner, farmer | Farmer → Mahindra Bolero; Business-owner → Toyota Innova Crysta. |
| **Religion / Caste / Ethnicity** | — | Limited but observed (e.g., auspicious car-purchase dates). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Demographic variables are the *easiest to measure* but are *behavioural predictors*, not behavioural variables themselves. Always note this distinction.

### 2.2.2 Psychographic Segmentation

Splits buyers by *lifestyle, values, attitudes, and personality* — the *why* behind buying.

| Psychographic Variable | Automotive Segment Example |
|---|---|
| **Lifestyle** (AIO — Activities, Interests, Opinions) | "Outdoorsy adventurer" → Jeep Wrangler, Thar. |
| **Social Class** | Upper class → BMW 7 Series, Audi A8. |
| **Personality** | Risk-takers → sports cars; Conformists → mass sedans. |
| **Values** | Eco-conscious → Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV. |
| **Attitudes** | Brand loyal → "I only buy Toyota"; Status-seeker → "I drive a Mercedes." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two famous psychographic classifications (high-yield for KTU):**
>
> 1. **VALS Framework** (Values, Attitudes, Lifestyles) — Mitchell & Bates (1981): divides US consumers into 8 types (Innovators, Thinkers, Achievers, Experiencers, Believers, Strivers, Makers, Survivors).
> 2. **AIO Inventory** — measures Activities, Interests, Opinions via questionnaire.
>
> In the Indian automotive context, *lifestyle segmentation* is increasingly used by Hyundai, Kia, MG.

### 2.2.3 Behavioural Segmentation

Splits buyers by *what they do* — actual behaviour, usage, or expected benefits.

| Behavioural Variable | Description | Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Benefits Sought** | Quality, economy, performance, safety, status, comfort. | Suzuki → mileage; Volvo → safety; BMW → performance/status. |
| **Usage Rate** | Light, medium, heavy user. | Fleet operators (Ola, Uber) → heavy users — bulk deals. |
| **User Status** | Non-user, ex-user, prospect, first-time, regular. | First-time buyers → Maruti Driving School, Alto, Swift. |
| **Loyalty Status** | Hard-core loyal, split loyal, shifting loyal, switcher. | Maruti's "Janta Car" loyalty; Hyundai's repeat-buyer schemes. |
| **Readiness Stage** | Unaware → Aware → Interested → Desire → Intend → Will buy. | CRM funnel used by every OEM. |
| **Occasion / Usage Situation** | Daily commute, weekend leisure, business trips, off-road, wedding. | Wedding-season SUV marketing in Punjab/Haryana. |

> [!TIP]
> **Marketers' Golden Rule (Kotler):** Behavioural segmentation is often the **best starting point** for a marketing plan because it directly reflects *customer needs*.

### 2.2.4 Geographic Segmentation

| Variable | Automotive Application |
|---|---|
| Region (North, South, East, West, NE) | Maruti strong in North; Hyundai strong in South. |
| City tier (Tier 1/2/3/rural) | Tier-1 → EVs; Rural → Bolero, Scorpio, used cars. |
| Climate / Terrain | Hill states → 4WD SUVs; Coastal → corrosion-resistant paints. |
| Cultural region | South-India has higher sedan preference. |

## 2.3 Targeting Strategies (The Four Ts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Once segments are identified, the firm must choose a *targeting strategy*. KTU expects all four strategies with **one automotive example each**.

| Strategy | Description | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Undifferentiated (Mass) Marketing** | Single offer for the whole market; ignores segment differences. | Henry Ford's Model T — *"any colour as long as it's black."* Today, very rare. |
| **2. Differentiated (Segmented) Marketing** | Design different offer for each segment entered. | Maruti has Alto, Swift, Dzire, Brezza, Ertiga, Grand Vitara — separate product for each segment. |
| **3. Concentrated (Niche) Marketing** | Focus on **one** segment only with deep specialisation. | Lamborghini → super-rich sports-car niche. MINI → premium compact retro niche in India. |
| **4. Micro-Marketing** | Tailoring programmes to **individuals** (mass customisation) or local areas. | Tesla's online-configurator; BMW Individual Manufaktur bespoke program. |

### 2.4 Evaluating Segment Attractiveness — The 3-C Model

Before selecting a target, evaluate each segment on three dimensions (Schiffman & Kanuk):

$$
\text{Segment Attractiveness} \;=\; f(\underbrace{\text{Segment Size \& Growth}}_{\text{Capacity}}, \underbrace{\text{Competitive Intensity}}_{\text{Competition}}, \underbrace{\text{Strategic Fit}}_{\text{Compatibility}})
$$

| Dimension | Question Asked | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Segment Capacity** | Is it large & growing? | Indian SUV market grew ~30% YoY in 2023-24. |
| **Competitive Intensity** | How intense is rivalry? | Entry-level hatchback segment is hyper-competitive. |
| **Strategic Fit** | Does it match our strengths? | Tesla's EV strength fits the urban eco-segment perfectly. |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| Concept / Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **STP** | Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning | Master framework. |
| **MADS-A Tests** | Measurable, Accessible, Differentiable, Substantial, Actionable | Effective-segment criteria. |
| **4 Segmentation Bases** | Demographic, Psychographic, Behavioural, Geographic | Always list all four. |
| **4 Targeting Strategies** | Undifferentiated, Differentiated, Concentrated, Micro | Always list all four. |
| **3-C Evaluation** | Capacity, Competition, Compatibility | Segment-attractiveness filter. |
| **VALS / AIO** | Psychographic tools | Lifestyle-segment classification. |
| **MAS** $\vert$ Macro Attractiveness Score $\vert$ | Weighted sum of segment characteristics | Used in advanced analyses. |

**Sample weighted attractiveness score (advanced) — useful for 14-mark answers:**

$$
S_i \;=\; w_1 \cdot \text{Size}_i + w_2 \cdot \text{Growth}_i + w_3 \cdot \text{Fit}_i - w_4 \cdot \text{Competition}_i
$$

where
- $S_i$ = Attractiveness score of segment $i$
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Strategically chosen weights, with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$
- $\text{Size}_i, \text{Growth}_i, \text{Fit}_i, \text{Competition}_i$ = Normalised scores (0–1)

> [!TIP]
> A segment is **viable** if $S_i \geq 0.5$ and is **preferred** if $S_i = \max(S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n)$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Product Design:** Inputs for engineering teams on which features to prioritise per segment.
- **Supply Chain:** Differentiation in dealer networks, service-centre density.
- **Finance:** Loan-tenure products (e.g., 84-month EMIs for compact-SUV Gen-Z buyers).
- **R&D:** Tata Research avoided early EV bets on premium segment after segment-attractiveness analysis showed family-car EVs were larger and more strategic.
- **Digital Marketing:** Programmatic ad-targeting uses demographic + behavioural signals.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section treats the segmentation-targeting problem as a **structured 6-step analytical process** (modified from Kotler & Keller, 16e). Each step is shown with explicit logical transitions, and the decision matrix is fully laid out for KTU application.

## 3.1 The Six-Step Segmentation-Targeting Process

### Step 1 — Define the Market

Clearly state the product-market boundary using the **"Product × Customer × Geography"** triple.

$$
\text{Market} = \{\,\text{Product} \times \text{Customer} \times \text{Geography}\,\}
$$

**Automotive Example:**
> *"The Indian passenger-car market, products = new 4-wheelers, customers = individuals & businesses, geography = India."*

### Step 2 — Identify Segmentation Variables

Select the **bases** (demographic, psychographic, behavioural, geographic) and the **variables** under each.

**Example selection matrix for Indian automobiles:**

| Base | Variable Chosen | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Demographic | Income, Age, Family size | Easy to measure via RTO/registration data. |
| Psychographic | Lifestyle, Values | Aligns with brand-positioning needs. |
| Behavioural | Benefit sought, Usage rate | Directly drives product-feature decisions. |
| Geographic | City-tier, Region | Dealer-network decisions. |

### Step 3 — Develop Segment Profiles

Construct a **rich descriptive profile** of each candidate segment.

> **Example — Segment A: "Young Urban Tech-Professional"**
> 
> *Demographic:* Age 25–35, single or DINK, income ₹10–25 LPA, metro-city resident, post-graduate.
> *Psychographic:* Tech-savvy, values convenience, environmentally aware, status-conscious.
> *Behavioural:* First-time premium buyer, research-led, prefers online booking, expects connected-car features.
> *Geography:* Bangalore, Mumbai, Delhi-NCR, Hyderabad, Pune.

### Step 4 — Evaluate Segment Attractiveness (MADS-A + 3-C)

Apply the **MADS-A tests** and the **3-C framework** (Capacity, Competition, Compatibility) to each candidate.

| Segment | Measurable? | Accessible? | Differentiable? | Substantial? | Actionable? | MADS-A Pass? | Size | Growth | Fit | Competition | 3-C Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A: Young Tech-Pro | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Yes** | Mid | High | High | High | **Choose** |
| B: Rural Farmer | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Yes** | Large | Low | High | Low | **Choose** |
| C: Luxury Executive | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Mid | ✓ | **Yes** | Mid | Mid | Mid | High | **Maybe** |
| D: Teen Two-Wheeler | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | **No** | — | — | — | — | **Reject** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Segment D fails:** It is not differentiable from the regular two-wheeler segment, and is not substantial because legal driving age is 18, so true teens are off-market.

### Step 5 — Select the Target Segments

Using the **attractiveness score formula** from §2.5:

$$
S_i = w_1 \cdot \text{Size}_i + w_2 \cdot \text{Growth}_i + w_3 \cdot \text{Fit}_i - w_4 \cdot \text{Competition}_i
$$

**Numerical demonstration:**

| Segment | Size (0–1) | Growth (0–1) | Fit (0–1) | Competition (0–1) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A: Young Tech-Pro | 0.6 | 0.9 | 0.9 | 0.7 |
| B: Rural Farmer | 0.9 | 0.3 | 0.9 | 0.2 |
| C: Luxury Exec | 0.4 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.8 |

Choose weights $w_1 = 0.20, \; w_2 = 0.30, \; w_3 = 0.30, \; w_4 = 0.20$.

**For Segment A (Young Tech-Pro):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.20)(0.6) + (0.30)(0.9) + (0.30)(0.9) - (0.20)(0.7) \\
&= 0.12 + 0.27 + 0.27 - 0.14 \\
&= 0.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Segment B (Rural Farmer):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.20)(0.9) + (0.30)(0.3) + (0.30)(0.9) - (0.20)(0.2) \\
&= 0.18 + 0.09 + 0.27 - 0.04 \\
&= 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Segment C (Luxury Executive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.20)(0.4) + (0.30)(0.5) + (0.30)(0.6) - (0.20)(0.8) \\
&= 0.08 + 0.15 + 0.18 - 0.16 \\
&= 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking:** $S_A = 0.52 \;>\; S_B = 0.50 \;>\; S_C = 0.25$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** Segments A and B are both **viable** ($S_i \geq 0.5$). A differentiated-targeting strategy is recommended: the firm should develop two separate marketing mixes, one for each segment.

### Step 6 — Develop Positioning (briefly, for completeness)

For each chosen target, craft a **positioning statement** using the **4W template**:

$$
\text{For} \;[\text{target customer}] \;\text{who} \;[\text{want/needs}], \;\text{our} \;[\text{brand}] \;\text{is} \;[\text{concept}] \;\text{that} \;[\text{key benefit}].
$$

**Example — Segment A positioning:**

> *"For young urban tech-professionals who want a connected, eco-friendly driving experience, the Tata Nexon EV is a smart, sustainable premium hatchback that delivers advanced technology, zero emissions, and instant torque — at an accessible price."*

## 3.2 Segment-Viability Decision Matrix (Master Reference)

| Score Range ($S_i$) | Verdict | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $S_i \geq 0.7$ | **Highly Attractive** | Primary target; aggressive investment. |
| $0.5 \leq S_i < 0.7$ | **Attractive** | Secondary target; selective investment. |
| $0.3 \leq S_i < 0.5$ | **Marginal** | Monitor; do not enter yet. |
| $S_i < 0.3$ | **Unattractive** | Reject; no resource allocation. |

## 3.3 Common Pitfalls Students Make in the Analytical Process

1. Confusing **segmentation variables** (income) with **segment members** (high-income millennials).
2. Skipping MADS-A — examiners actively deduct marks.
3. Using **single-base** segmentation (e.g., only demographic) and calling it complete.
4. Forgetting to **link** targeting strategy to the firm's resources and capabilities.
5. Ignoring **behavioural segmentation** in a question that specifically asks for it.
