# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define market segmentation. State the criteria for effective market segmentation.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> **Market Segmentation** is the process of **dividing a large heterogeneous market into smaller, homogeneous sub-groups** of consumers who share similar needs, characteristics, or behaviours, and who are likely to respond similarly to marketing actions. *(1 mark)*

> **Criteria for Effective Segmentation (Kotler's Five Tests — MADS-A):** *(2 marks)*
> 1. **Measurable** — size, profile, and purchasing power can be quantified.
> 2. **Accessible** — the segment can be reached and served effectively.
> 3. **Differentiable** — segments are conceptually distinct and respond differently to marketing mixes.
> 4. **Substantial** — large and profitable enough to serve.
> 5. **Actionable** — effective marketing programmes can be designed to attract and serve the segment.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**Explain behavioural segmentation with reference to the automotive industry.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> **Behavioural segmentation** divides buyers on the basis of their **actual behaviour, usage patterns, benefits sought, loyalty status, and readiness to buy.** *(1 mark)*

> **Automotive examples:** *(2 marks)*
> 1. **Benefits Sought** — Suzuki markets mileage; Volvo markets safety; BMW markets performance.
> 2. **Usage Rate** — Fleet operators (Ola, Uber) are heavy users; individuals use cars occasionally.
> 3. **User Status** — First-time buyers targeted by Maruti Alto/Swift; repeat-buyers targeted by brand-loyalty programmes.
> 4. **Loyalty** — Maruti's *Janta Car* loyalty drives 60% repeat-purchase behaviour.
> 5. **Occasion** — SUVs marketed heavily in Punjab/Haryana during the wedding season.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Part (a)** — *Explain the various bases of market segmentation. Discuss in detail how demographic, psychographic, and behavioural segmentation are applied in the Indian automotive market. (7 marks)*

**Part (b)** — *Compare and contrast the four targeting strategies available to an automobile manufacturer. With suitable examples, recommend a strategy for an EV start-up entering India. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Segmentation bases are the variables used to slice the market. Kotler identifies four major bases: **Demographic, Psychographic, Behavioural, and Geographic**. The Indian automotive market uses all four extensively.

**Demographic Segmentation (2 Marks):**
- **Age:** Gen-Z (18–25) buys entry-hatchbacks like Maruti Alto; Millennials (26–40) prefer compact SUVs like Brezza and Nexon; Boomers (55+) buy comfort sedans like Camry.
- **Income:** Clear ladder from Alto (₹4L) to Mercedes S-Class (₹1.7 Cr).
- **Family Life Cycle:** Newly married → compact sedan; Full-nest → 7-seater (Innova, Safari).
- **Gender:** Women-targeted compact SUVs such as Tata Punch *Pride* and Hyundai Exter.

**Psychographic Segmentation (2 Marks):**
- **Lifestyle AIO:** "Outdoorsy adventurer" → Thar/Wrangler; "Status-seeker" → BMW/Mercedes.
- **Values:** Eco-conscious → Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV.
- **Personality & Social Class:** Risk-takers buy sports cars; conformists buy mass-sedans.
- **Tools used:** VALS, AIO inventory, Big Five personality trait mapping.

**Behavioural Segmentation (2 Marks):**
- **Benefits Sought:** Mileage (Maruti), safety (Volvo, Tata), performance (BMW), status (Mercedes).
- **Usage Rate:** Fleet buyers (Ola, Uber) — heavy users; families — moderate.
- **Loyalty & Readiness:** Maruti's 60% repeat rate; CRM-driven readiness-stage targeting.
- **Occasion:** Wedding-season SUV marketing in Punjab/Haryana.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 Marks)

**Four Targeting Strategies (4 Marks):**

| Strategy | Description | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Undifferentiated** | One offer for the entire market. | Henry Ford's Model T. |
| **Differentiated** | Different offers for different segments. | Maruti: Alto + Swift + Dzire + Brezza + Grand Vitara. |
| **Concentrated (Niche)** | Deep focus on one narrow segment. | Lamborghini, MINI Cooper. |
| **Micro-Marketing** | Individual/location-level customisation. | Tesla configurator, BMW Individual. |

**Recommendation for an EV Start-up Entering India (3 Marks):**
A new EV start-up with limited capital should adopt a **Concentrated (Niche) Targeting** strategy, focusing on the **"Young Urban Tech-Professional Early-Adopter"** segment in Tier-1 cities. Justification:
- Segment is **measurable** (RTO + LinkedIn data).
- Segment is **substantial** (6 lakh+ early-adopter EV buyers in metros).
- Segment is **differentiable** (tech-eco-conscious, low brand-loyalty).
- Firm can achieve **strong positioning** before scaling to a **Differentiated** strategy in 3–5 years.
- *Example in practice:* Ather Energy initially concentrated on Bangalore tech-professionals before expanding pan-India.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**Part (a)** — *What are the criteria for effective market segmentation? Evaluate an Indian passenger-car manufacturer's market segments using the MADS-A framework. (7 marks)*

**Part (b)** — *Discuss psychographic segmentation in detail. How is it used in designing marketing mixes for premium automobiles in India? (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks)

**MADS-A Criteria (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Meaning | India Car-Market Application |
|---|---|---|
| **M**easurable | Quantifiable | Income (RTO data) — "₹10–25 LPA buyers" measurable. |
| **A**ccessible | Reachable | Metro dealership networks; digital ads. |
| **D**ifferentiable | Distinct from others | SUV buyers ≠ sedan buyers in needs. |
| **S**ubstantial | Profitable size | 6 Lakh premium-SUV buyers/year in Tier-1. |
| **A**ctionable | Programmes can be designed | EV-charging + fast-service network. |

**Evaluation of Three Segments Using MADS-A (4 Marks):**

| Segment | M | A | D | S | A | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Urban Tech-Pro (Nexon EV buyers) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Select** |
| Rural SUV buyers (Bolero) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Select** |
| Teen Compact buyers | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | **Reject** (legal age, similar to general two-wheeler segment) |

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks)

**Psychographic Segmentation — Definition (1 Mark):**
Psychographic segmentation divides buyers on the basis of **lifestyle, values, attitudes, and personality** — the inner psychological drivers of purchase behaviour.

**Variables Used (3 Marks):**
- **Lifestyle (AIO):** Activities, Interests, Opinions.
- **Social Class:** Upper, middle, lower.
- **Personality:** Innovator, achiever, experiencer.
- **VALS Framework:** 8 lifestyle categories.
- **Big Five Personality Traits:** Openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism.

**Use in Premium-Automobile Marketing Mix (3 Marks):**
| Element | Application for Premium Buyers |
|---|---|
| **Product** | Bespoke interiors, premium leather, advanced driver-assistance. |
| **Price** | Value-based pricing, not cost-plus — appeals to status-seekers. |
| **Place** | Exclusive dealership showrooms; white-glove delivery. |
| **Promotion** | Lifestyle magazines, invite-only events, brand-experience centres. |

**Examples:** BMW emphasises "Joy of Driving" (lifestyle); Mercedes emphasises "The Best or Nothing" (values); Audi emphasises "Vorsprung durch Technik" (personality of the forward-thinker).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (avoid these traps):**
> 1. **Conflating STP:** Students often jump to *Positioning* without completing Segmentation + Targeting. Marks are awarded **stage-by-stage**. Skipping = −3 to −4 marks.
> 2. **Single-base answers:** A 7-mark question explicitly asking for "demographic, psychographic, behavioural" must show **all three with examples**. Single-base = max 3 marks.
> 3. **No real-world example:** Without an automotive example (e.g., Maruti, Tata, Hyundai), the answer remains abstract. Always pair theory with at least **one Indian example per base**.
> 4. **Forgetting the formula/matrix:** Even in management subjects, showing the **MADS-A matrix** or **3-C score** demonstrates depth — examiners love it.
> 5. **Confusing "targeting" with "positioning":** Targeting = *choosing the segment*. Positioning = *what the brand stands for* in that segment. Mix them up = −2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorise This Block:**

- **STP** = **S**egmentation → **T**argeting → **P**ositioning.
- **Segmentation** = slicing the heterogeneous market into homogeneous sub-groups.
- **Targeting** = selecting which sub-group(s) to serve.
- **Four Bases:** Demographic, Psychographic, Behavioural, Geographic.
- **Five Tests (MADS-A):** Measurable, Accessible, Differentiable, Substantial, Actionable.
- **Four Targeting Strategies:** Undifferentiated, Differentiated, Concentrated, Micro.
- **3-C Evaluation:** Capacity, Competition, Compatibility.
- **Indian Automotive Examples to Remember:**
  - Maruti → mass market, mileage (behavioural — benefit).
  - Tata Nexon EV → eco-conscious (psychographic — value).
  - Innova Crysta → full-nest family (demographic — life-cycle).
  - MINI Cooper → niche concentrated targeting.
  - Maruti Alto → first-time-buyer behavioural targeting.
- **Behavioural segmentation is the most action-oriented base** — Kotler's golden rule.
- **Demographic is the most measurable but least explanatory base.**
- **Psychographic is the deepest but hardest to measure** — needs primary research.
- **Attractiveness formula:** $S_i = w_1 \text{Size} + w_2 \text{Growth} + w_3 \text{Fit} - w_4 \text{Competition}$.
- **Decision Rule:** $S_i \geq 0.5 \Rightarrow$ **Viable**; $S_i \geq 0.7 \Rightarrow$ **Highly Attractive**.
- **Differentiated strategy** is the most common in the Indian car market (Maruti, Hyundai, Tata).
- **Concentrated strategy** is suited for new entrants with limited capital (start-up EVs, niche sports cars).
- **Micro-marketing** is growing rapidly via connected cars, online configurators, and telematics-based personalisation.
- **Linkage:** Segmentation + Targeting *must precede* Marketing Mix design — never reverse the order in a KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Lifecycle Management - Understanding the Stages of the Automotive Product Lifecycle and Strategies for Managing Products at Each Stage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Lifecycle Management - Understanding the Stages of the Automotive Product Lifecycle and Strategies for Managing Products at Each Stage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** is the strategic marketing process of tracking a product from its inception through design, development, market introduction, growth, maturity, and eventual decline, combined with the coordinated business strategies deployed to optimize revenue, market share, and profitability at every phase. The **Automotive Product Lifecycle (Auto-PLC)** is the industry-specific application of this framework, tracking an automobile model (e.g., Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta, Tata Nexon) from concept ideation, R&D, market launch, sales acceleration, saturation, and discontinuation or facelift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim-aligned):**
> The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is a model that describes the **stages a product goes through from when it is first introduced to the market until it is withdrawn**, comprising four (or five) sequential stages: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline** — with some authors including a pre-launch "Development" stage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a car model like a **human life**:

| Human Life Stage | Automotive Product Equivalent | Key Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Infancy (Birth)** | Introduction (e.g., New Tata Punch EV launch) | Slow growth, high investment, low awareness |
| **Childhood (Growth spurt)** | Growth (e.g., Hyundai Creta after 2015) | Rapid sales rise, competitors enter |
| **Adulthood (Peak career)** | Maturity (e.g., Maruti Swift, Maruti Alto) | Sales plateau, maximum profit, intense competition |
| **Old Age (Retirement)** | Decline (e.g., Maruti Omni, Tata Indigo) | Falling sales, phasing out, facelifts to revive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Automotive Example (KTU-style case anchor):**
> Consider the **Toyota Innova**. Launched in 2005, it saw rapid Growth, peaked in Maturity around 2014-2018, then faced Decline due to UV/MPV competition (Kia Carens, Maruti Ertiga). Toyota responded with the **Innova Crysta (2016)** and **Innova HyCross (2022)** — classic PLC extension and revival strategies.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

The following metrics are universally tracked by automotive OEMs at every PLC stage:

- **Sales Volume (Units Sold)** — measured in absolute numbers or YoY % change
- **Market Share (%)** — brand's share of total category sales
- **Revenue (₹ Crores / $ Billions)** — top-line financial measure
- **Gross Profit Margin (%)** — profitability efficiency
- **Customer Adoption Rate (%)** — new buyer acquisition
- **Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI)** — efficiency of marketing spend
- **Production Volume vs. Capacity Utilization (%)** — supply-side indicator
- **Days of Supply / Inventory Days** — dealer health metric

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently frame questions around **"what strategy should a marketer adopt at the Maturity stage?"** — the answer is always a mix of **Product Modification, Price Adjustment, Distribution Intensification, and Promotional Repositioning** (the classic 4P recalibration).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Stages of the Automotive PLC — Structured Logic Breakdown

#### **Stage 1: Introduction (Pre-Launch + Launch Phase)**

**Why it happens:** The OEM has completed R&D, homologation, and dealer readiness. The market has no prior awareness of the model.

**How it behaves:**
- Sales volume is **low** and **slow-growing**
- Heavy **promotional spend** (advertising, launch events, influencer tie-ups)
- Pricing is typically **penetration pricing** (low to grab share) or **skimming pricing** (high for early adopters like Tesla Cybertruck)
- Distribution is **selective** — flagship dealerships in metros
- Product is offered in **limited variants**

**Automotive Examples:** Tata Punch EV (2023 launch), Mahindra XUV.e8 (2024 launch), MG Comet EV (2023)

#### **Stage 2: Growth (Market Acceleration Phase)**

**Why it happens:** Product gains acceptance, word-of-mouth kicks in, competitors enter.

**How it behaves:**
- Sales rise **exponentially**
- Profits increase as **economies of scale** kick in
- **Product line extensions** (new variants, trims, colours) introduced
- Distribution becomes **intensive** — expanded dealer network
- **Competitive entry** — rival launches (e.g., when Creta grew, Seltos, Kushaq, Taigun entered)
- Promotional spend is **sustained** but efficiency improves

#### **Stage 3: Maturity (Saturation Phase) — The Longest Stage**

**Why it happens:** Most adopters who would buy have bought. Market saturates. Competition is intense.

**How it behaves:**
- Sales **plateau**, growth flattens
- **Profit peaks** (highest absolute profit)
- Intense **price competition** and discount wars
- **Product modifications** — facelifts, feature upgrades, new editions
- Defending market share becomes the focus
- New segments/sub-segments targeted

**Automotive Examples:** Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta, Maruti Baleno — all in prolonged maturity

#### **Stage 4: Saturation (Peak) — Sub-stage**

- Sales reach **absolute peak**
- Inventory pile-up risks emerge
- Aggressive **clearance offers**, exchange bonuses, finance schemes

#### **Stage 5: Decline (Discontinuation Phase)**

**Why it happens:** Technology obsolescence (BS6 phased out older norms), shifting consumer preferences (sedans declining in India post-SUV wave), or cannibalization by own new model.

**How it behaves:**
- Sales **drop steadily**
- Profit erodes
- Options: **Harvest** (milk remaining cash), **Discontinue**, or **Relaunch (with changes)**

**Automotive Examples:** Maruti Omni (discontinued 2019), Tata Indigo (discontinued), Honda Jazz (discontinued India 2021), Ford Aspire (2021)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Strategic Cheat Sheet

| **PLC Stage** | **Sales Curve** | **Pricing Strategy** | **Promotion Focus** | **Distribution** | **Product Strategy** | **Profit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduction** | Low, slow rise | Penetration / Skimming | High awareness-building | Selective / Exclusive | Basic offering | Negative / Low |
| **Growth** | Rapid rise | Maintained / slight reduction | Differentiation, brand-building | Intensive expansion | Line extensions, variants | Rising |
| **Maturity** | Plateau (peak) | Competitive / discount | Reminder + repositioning | Maximum intensity | Modifications, facelifts | **Peak** |
| **Saturation** | Flat at peak | Aggressive discounts | Loyalty schemes | Maintain | Differentiation through features | Plateau |
| **Decline** | Falling | Harvest pricing | Minimal | Phased reduction | Phase out OR re-launch | Falling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction (frequently asked):** Maturity ≠ Decline. Maturity means **sales are stable but profit is still high**; Decline means **sales are dropping and profit is eroding**. Conflating these is a 2-mark loss in valuation.

### 2.3 Real-World Strategic Utility

In the automotive industry, PLC management determines:

- **Platform decisions** (e.g., Maruti's Heartect platform shared across multiple models to extend lifecycle efficiency)
- **Facelift cadences** (Hyundai refreshes Creta every 3 years)
- **Brand portfolio pruning** (Ford exited India in 2021 — classic decline-stage harvest decision)
- **EV transition strategy** (Tata's early-mover PLC redefinition — converting existing platforms to EVs to manage ICE decline)
- **Used-car and service revenue extension** (the "aftermarket lifecycle" is a profit tail in the decline stage)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The PLC Curve — Mathematical Representation

The sales-vs-time relationship of an automotive product follows an **S-curve (logistic growth function)**. While we won't solve a calculus-based ODE, KTU expects you to articulate the **functional form and its strategic implications**.

The standard **logistic growth model** is:

$$S(t) \;=\; \frac{K}{1 \;+\; e^{-r(t - t_0)}}$$

Where the variables are defined as:

$$\begin{aligned}
S(t) &= \text{Sales volume at time } t \quad [\text{units}] \\
K &= \text{Saturation ceiling (market carrying capacity)} \quad [\text{units}] \\
r &= \text{Inherent growth-rate constant} \quad [\text{per year}] \\
t_0 &= \text{Inflection point (time of maximum growth rate)} \quad [\text{years}] \\
e &= \text{Euler's constant} \approx 2.71828
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Strategic Derivations for Each Stage

#### **3.2.1 Introduction Stage Strategic Derivations**

**Step 1: Identify market position**
$$\text{Position} = f(\text{Awareness}, \text{Distribution}, \text{Trial}, \text{Adoption})$$

**Step 2: Choose pricing model**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Penetration Price} &= \text{Variable Cost} \;+\; \text{Low Margin} \quad \rightarrow \text{fast volume} \\
\text{Skimmed Price} &= \text{Perceived Value} \times (1 + \text{Premium \%}) \quad \rightarrow \text{fast margin}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate launch break-even**
$$\text{Break-even Units} \;=\; \frac{\text{Fixed Launch Costs} \;+\; \text{Development Cost}}{\text{Unit Contribution Margin}}$$

> **Engineering Note (Automotive context):** Fixed launch costs include factory tooling (typically ₹2,000–5,000 Cr for a new platform), homologation, dealer setup, and launch marketing.

#### **3.2.2 Growth Stage Strategic Derivations**

**Step 1: Demand acceleration**
$$\text{Sales Growth Rate} \;=\; \frac{S_{t} - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2: Competitive response trigger**
$$\text{New Entrants} \;=\; f(\text{Profitability}, \text{Barrier Height}, \text{Imitability})$$

**Step 3: Profit scaling via economies of scale**
$$\text{Unit Cost} \;=\; \text{Fixed Cost} \;/\; \text{Volume} \;+\; \text{Variable Cost per Unit}$$

As volume rises, fixed cost spreads → **unit cost falls → margin expands → price can be reduced or profit can grow**.

#### **3.2.3 Maturity Stage Strategic Derivations**

**Step 1: Market share defence ratio**
$$\text{Defensive ROMI} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \text{Share} \times \text{Lifetime Value}}{\text{Marketing Spend}}$$

**Step 2: Facelift / Modification ROI**
$$\text{Modification ROI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Revenue Lift from Facelift} - \text{Facelift Cost}}{\text{Facelift Cost}}$$

**Step 3: Extension strategy mix**
$$\text{Maturity Strategy Mix} \;=\; \alpha \cdot \text{Product} \;+\; \beta \cdot \text{Price} \;+\; \gamma \cdot \text{Place} \;+\; \delta \cdot \text{Promotion}$$

where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 1$ and weights are set by management based on competitive audit.

#### **3.2.4 Decline Stage Strategic Derivations**

**Step 1: Harvest vs. Divest decision**
$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Harvest} & \text{if } \text{Cash Flow} > 0 \text{ and no strategic substitute} \\ \text{Divest/Discontinue} & \text{if } \text{Cash Flow} \leq 0 \text{ or cannibalization risk} \\ \text{Relaunch} & \text{if } \text{Market Revival Feasibility Score} > \text{Threshold} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Decline velocity assessment**
$$\text{Decline Rate} \;=\; \frac{S_{t} - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100\% \quad [\text{should be negative}]$$

**Step 3: Aftermarket pivot**
$$\text{Service Revenue Share} \;=\; \frac{\text{Aftermarket Parts + Service + Accessories}}{\text{Total Brand Revenue}} \times 100\%$$

> Many OEMs (e.g., Maruti) sustain declining models' **service revenue** for **5–7 years post-discontinuation**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stage Diagnosis Indicators

| **Indicator** | **Introduction** | **Growth** | **Maturity** | **Decline** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales trend | Slow rise | **Steep rise** | Plateau | Falling |
| Customer type | Innovators (2.5%) | Early majority (34%) | Late majority (34%) | Laggards (16%) |
| Competitor count | Few / none | Rising | Maximum | Falling |
| Profit | Negative | Rising | **Peak** | Eroding |
| Marketing spend | Very high | High | Stabilizing | Minimal |
| Product variants | Few | Many | Maximum | Pruned |
| Typical duration | 6 months – 2 years | 2–5 years | **5–15 years** | 1–5 years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation curve (Innovators → Early Adopters → Early Majority → Late Majority → Laggards) directly maps to the PLC stages. A 7-mark question may ask you to **link Rogers' curve to the PLC and explain the strategic implications**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The PLC Curve — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage1 Introduction] --> B[Stage2 Growth]
    B --> C[Stage3 Maturity]
    C --> D[Stage4 Saturation Peak]
    D --> E[Stage5 Decline]
    E --> F{Strategic Decision}
    F -->|Harvest| G[Milk residual cash flow]
    F -->|Discontinue| H[Phase out model]
    F -->|Relaunch| I[New variant or facelift]
    I --> B
```

### 4.2 Strategic Levers per Stage — Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTRO[INTRODUCTION STAGE]
        I1[High ad spend]
        I2[Penetration or skimming pricing]
        I3[Selective distribution]
        I4[Basic product line]
    end
    subgraph GROW[GROWTH STAGE]
        G1[Brand differentiation]
        G2[Line extensions]
        G3[Intensive distribution]
        G4[Aggressive promotion]
    end
    subgraph MAT[MATURITY STAGE]
        M1[Facelifts and modifications]
        M2[Price competition]
        M3[Loyalty programs]
        M4[New market segments]
    end
    subgraph DEC[DECLINE STAGE]
        D1[Harvest strategy]
        D2[Discontinue or pivot]
        D3[Aftermarket focus]
        D4[Reinvest in new models]
    end
    INTRO --> GROW
    GROW --> MAT
    MAT --> DEC
```

### 4.3 Sales-vs-Time Schematic (Automotive PLC Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Launch point low sales] -->|Slow growth| B[Steep rise growth phase]
    B -->|Plateau formation| C[Peak sales maturity]
    C -->|Stable flat| D[Saturation peak]
    D -->|Downward slope| E[Declining sales]
    E -->|End of life| F[Discontinuation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic for the Exam:** The PLC curve looks like a **stretched-out letter "S"** with a long downward tail. Remember: **S = Sales that Start, Soar, Saturate, and Sink**.

### 4.4 The 4P Recalibration Matrix Across Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STG[PLC STAGE]
        S1[Introduction]
        S2[Growth]
        S3[Maturity]
        S4[Decline]
    end
    subgraph PROD[PRODUCT LEVER]
        P1[Basic]
        P2[Extended]
        P3[Differentiated]
        P4[Harvested]
    end
    subgraph PRICE[PRICE LEVER]
        PR1[Skim or Penetrate]
        PR2[Stable]
        PR3[Discount]
        PR4[Cut]
    end
    subgraph PLACE[PLACE LEVER]
        PL1[Selective]
        PL2[Intensive]
        PL3[Saturated]
        PL4[Phased out]
    end
    subgraph PROMO[PROMOTION LEVER]
        PM1[Informative heavy]
        PM2[Persuasive]
        PM3[Reminder]
        PM4[Minimal]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S1 --> PR1
    S1 --> PL1
    S1 --> PM1
    S2 --> P2
    S2 --> PR2
    S2 --> PL2
    S2 --> PM2
    S3 --> P3
    S3 --> PR3
    S3 --> PL3
    S3 --> PM3
    S4 --> P4
    S4 --> PR4
    S4 --> PL4
    S4 --> PM4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the Product Life Cycle (PLC). List its four main stages.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is a marketing model that describes the stages a product passes through from its introduction into the market until its withdrawal. It helps marketers anticipate changes in sales, profit, and competition, and design appropriate strategies.

The **four main stages** are:

1. **Introduction** — product is launched, sales are low, heavy promotional spending
2. **Growth** — rapid sales increase, competitors enter, profits rise
3. **Maturity** — sales plateau at peak, profit maximum, intense competition
4. **Decline** — sales fall, profit erodes, decision to harvest, discontinue, or relaunch

> *(Examiner's note: A clean 4-point list with the definition earns full 3 marks. Missing the definition costs 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Identify and briefly explain the maturity stage strategies used for an automobile.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Maturity stage** is the longest phase of the automotive PLC, where sales plateau and profit is at its peak. The four key strategies are:

1. **Product Modification (Facelift)** — introducing design refreshes, new features, and updated technology (e.g., Hyundai Creta facelift every 3 years)
2. **Price Adjustment** — competitive pricing, exchange bonuses, finance offers to retain buyers
3. **Distribution Intensification** — expanding dealer reach into Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities
4. **Promotional Repositioning** — shifting communication to lifestyle, safety, or aspirational messaging rather than awareness

> *(Examiner's note: At least 3 of the 4 strategies must be clearly stated with an automotive example for full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a) Explain the five stages of the Product Life Cycle with reference to the Indian automotive industry. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the strategic options available to a marketer when a product enters the decline stage. Use an automotive example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Stages of PLC with Indian Automotive Examples (7 marks):**

The PLC is a strategic model that maps the sales and profit trajectory of a product over time. Applied to the Indian automotive industry, the five stages are:

1. **Introduction Stage:** The product is newly launched with low awareness and slow sales. The OEM invests heavily in marketing and dealer setup. *Example:* **MG Comet EV (2023)** — launched in a niche micro-EV segment with selective distribution and high promotional spend.

2. **Growth Stage:** Sales rise rapidly as the product gains acceptance, competitors enter, and product line extensions are introduced. *Example:* **Hyundai Creta (2015–2019)** — saw exponential sales, multiple variants, and triggered rivals like Kia Seltos, VW Taigun, and Skoda Kushaq.

3. **Maturity Stage:** Sales plateau at a high level, profit peaks, and competition is intense. The focus shifts to defending market share through facelifts and variants. *Example:* **Maruti Swift** — has been in extended maturity for over a decade with periodic facelifts and ZXi+/ZXi+ variants.

4. **Saturation Stage:** The market is fully penetrated; growth is essentially zero and inventory pile-ups may occur. *Example:* **Maruti Alto (peak years 2010–2015)** — was the top-selling car for years before saturation.

5. **Decline Stage:** Sales fall due to changing consumer preferences, technology obsolescence, or cannibalization. *Example:* **Maruti Omni** — discontinued in 2019 due to safety and emission norm changes.

**(b) Strategic Options in the Decline Stage (7 marks):**

When an automotive product enters decline, the marketer has four strategic options:

1. **Harvesting:** Reduce marketing spend and continue selling to milk residual cash flow, often relying on service revenue. *Example:* **Tata Indigo** — was harvested before eventual discontinuation.

2. **Discontinuation / Divestment:** Exit the market entirely. *Example:* **Ford India (2021)** — exited manufacturing, relying on service for existing customers.

3. **Relaunch / Rejuvenation:** Reposition with new features, target a new segment, or convert to a new powertrain. *Example:* **Tata Nexon EV** — built on the platform of the declining Nexon diesel/petrol trend, creating a new growth cycle.

4. **Pivot to Aftermarket:** Continue producing parts, service, and accessories to support the installed base. *Example:* **Maruti Omni** service parts still available years after discontinuation.

The decision depends on **brand equity, installed base, cannibalization risk, and strategic fit** with the OEM's portfolio.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Alternative]**
**(a) Describe the Product Life Cycle concept. How is it different from the diffusion of innovation curve? (7 marks)**
**(b) "Marketing strategies must change at every stage of the PLC." Discuss this statement in the context of the automotive industry. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PLC vs. Diffusion of Innovation (7 marks):**

**Product Life Cycle (PLC):**
The PLC is a **sales-oriented** model that describes a product's sales and profit trajectory from launch to withdrawal across four/five stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline. It focuses on **what the firm experiences** — sales volume, profit, competition, and marketing spend.

**Diffusion of Innovation (Rogers' Curve):**
The Diffusion of Innovation is a **consumer-adoption-oriented** model that classifies buyers by their willingness to adopt a new product: **Innovators (2.5%), Early Adopters (13.5%), Early Majority (34%), Late Majority (34%), and Laggards (16%)**. It focuses on **how customers adopt** the innovation over time.

**Key Differences:**

| **Aspect** | **PLC** | **Diffusion of Innovation** |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Sales/Profit trajectory | Customer adoption behaviour |
| Origin | Marketing management | Sociology/Rural studies |
| Stages | 4–5 commercial stages | 5 adopter categories |
| Curve shape | S-curve over product lifetime | Bell-curve over adoption |
| Strategic use | Marketing mix calibration | Targeting and segmentation |

**Connection:** The two curves are complementary — the PLC's *Introduction* stage corresponds to *Innovators + Early Adopters*; the *Growth* stage corresponds to *Early Majority*; *Maturity* to *Late Majority*; and *Decline* to *Laggards*.

**(b) Marketing Strategies Must Change at Every PLC Stage (7 marks):**

This statement is **absolutely true**, and the automotive industry provides a strong illustration:

1. **Introduction:** Strategy focuses on **awareness creation** — heavy advertising, influencer campaigns, selective dealer launch. *Example:* Mahindra XUV.e8 launch with celebrity endorsements and digital-first campaign.

2. **Growth:** Strategy shifts to **differentiation and expansion** — broader variant portfolio, wider dealer network, competitive pricing, and brand-building. *Example:* Tata Nexon EV expanding variants (LR, MR, Empowered+) post-launch.

3. **Maturity:** Strategy emphasizes **defence and modification** — facelifts, feature additions, financing schemes, and exchange bonuses. *Example:* Maruti Swift facelift every 3 years with new infotainment and ADAS features.

4. **Decline:** Strategy turns to **harvest, divest, or rejuvenate** — cost reduction, service focus, or platform migration. *Example:* Ford's exit from India, Maruti's phase-out of the Omni.

**Conclusion:** A single static marketing strategy across the PLC would result in **missed opportunities in growth, over-investment in decline, and under-investment in introduction**. Hence, dynamic strategy recalibration is essential — this is the cornerstone of **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common 2-mark loss:** Confusing *Maturity* with *Decline* — they are different stages with different sales trends.
> - **Common 3-mark loss:** Listing PLC stages without **automotive-specific examples** — KTU requires industry contextualization.
> - **Common 2-mark loss:** Failing to mention **all 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion)** when asked about stage strategies.
> - **Common 3-mark loss:** Treating PLC and Diffusion of Innovation as the **same model** — they are complementary but distinct.
> - **Common 2-mark loss:** Writing "PLC is for products" without specifying it tracks **sales and profit trajectory** — vague answers lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PLC = Product Life Cycle** — tracks sales and profit from launch to withdrawal.
- [x] **Four main stages:** Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline (some models add Saturation or Development).
- [x] **Maturity is the longest stage** and has the **peak profit**, not the peak sales (peak sales is saturation).
- [x] **Introduction:** Low sales, high cost, negative/low profit, heavy promotion, selective distribution.
- [x] **Growth:** Rapid sales rise, competitors enter, line extensions, intensive distribution.
- [x] **Maturity:** Sales plateau, profit peak, intense competition, facelifts and modifications.
- [x] **Decline:** Sales fall, profit erodes, options = Harvest / Discontinue / Relaunch / Pivot to Aftermarket.
- [x] **Automotive examples:** Swift (Maturity), Creta (Maturity), Punch EV (Introduction), Omni (Decline), Innova (Maturity→Decline).
- [x] **Strategies are 4P-driven:** Product, Price, Place, Promotion — each stage requires a different mix.
- [x] **PLC curve = S-shaped (logistic function):** $S(t) = \frac{K}{1 + e^{-r(t - t_0)}}$.
- [x] **PLC ≠ Diffusion of Innovation** — PLC is sales-based; DOI is customer-adoption-based.
- [x] **PLM = Product Lifecycle Management** — the strategic orchestration of marketing across all PLC stages.
- [x] **Facelift cadence in Indian auto industry** is typically **3 years**.
- [x] **Service/aftermarket revenue** is a key **decline-stage profit tail**.
- [x] **Always include an automotive example** in KTU 7-mark answers to fetch full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Product Development - Techniques and Processes for Developing New Automotive Products, Including Market Research, Concept Development and Product Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** New Product Development - Techniques and Processes for Developing New Automotive Products, Including Market Research, Concept Development and Product Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**New Product Development (NPD)** in the automotive industry is defined as the **structured, cross-functional, end-to-end process of conceiving, designing, prototyping, testing, commercialising, and continuously improving a vehicle, sub-system, or service that is perceived by the customer as materially new.** It encompasses every stage from initial opportunity identification through to market launch, dealership ramp-up, and post-launch product lifecycle management.

According to the **Product Development Management Association (PDMA) framework**, NPD is the complete journey from idea generation to market introduction, governed by formal Stage-Gate models. In an **Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)** context such as Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, Toyota, or Tesla, NPD integrates three overlapping streams:

1. **Marketing Stream** – Customer needs, segmentation, positioning, pricing.
2. **Design/Engineering Stream** – Styling, CAD/CAE, powertrain, electronics, chassis.
3. **Manufacturing Stream** – Process planning, tooling, supply chain readiness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly expects the student to know NPD **not as a linear waterfall but as a parallel, iterative funnel** that combines *market research → concept development → product testing*. Examiners frequently award marks for mentioning the **Stage-Gate** terminology.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a famous chef is launching an entirely new dish for a Michelin-starred restaurant. He does **not** just walk into the kitchen and start cooking. Instead:

- **Market Research** = He first surveys customers, checks seasonal ingredients, studies competitor menus, and reads food critic blogs.
- **Concept Development** = He sketches a flavour profile, writes a recipe idea, and creates a "mock photo" of the plate.
- **Concept Testing** = He serves a tiny tasting portion to 20 trusted regulars and asks, *"Would you pay ₹800 for this?"*
- **Prototype (Alpha Test)** = He cooks 5 full versions in his own test kitchen with different spice levels.
- **Product Testing (Beta Test)** = He puts it on the menu for one week as a "Chef's Special" and gathers feedback.
- **Launch (Gamma Test)** = The dish becomes permanent, staff are trained, suppliers are locked in.

An automotive OEM like **Tata Motors launching the Punch EV** or **Toyota launching the Urban Cruiser Taisor** follows the **exact same disciplined sequence**, just scaled up to billions of rupees and 4–7 years of effort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Automotive NPD Metric:** A typical passenger-car NPD cycle is **36 to 84 months** (3 to 7 years). The industry benchmark for "Time-to-Market" is often measured in **months from programme start (M0) to Start of Production (SOP)**.

### 1.3 Why NPD is Strategically Critical for an OEM

- **Survival:** Average automotive product lifecycle has shrunk from **10 years (2000)** to roughly **6–7 years (2024)** due to EV disruption and software-defined vehicles.
- **Differentiation:** Roughly **30–40 % of an OEM's revenue** comes from products launched in the previous 3 years.
- **Risk Mitigation:** A failed launch (e.g., a poorly-tested ADAS feature) can trigger recalls costing **billions of dollars** in warranty and brand damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven-Stage Automotive NPD Framework (Cooper's Stage-Gate® Adapted for OEMs)

The **Stage-Gate process**, originally developed by **Dr. Robert G. Cooper**, is the **de facto global standard** used by almost every automotive OEM and Tier-1 supplier. Each stage produces a deliverable, and each gate is a **go / kill / recycle / hold decision** made by a cross-functional Programme Steering Committee (PSC).

#### Stage 1 — Idea Generation (Discovery)
- **Inputs:** Voice of Customer (VoC), dealer feedback, warranty data, competitor teardowns, technology roadmaps (battery, ADAS, materials), regulatory trends (BS-VII, CAFE, Euro 7).
- **Tools:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, TRIZ, ethnographic shadowing (e.g., observing Indian middle-class families loading a Maruti Ertiga on a holiday).
- **Output:** A pool of 50–150 raw ideas per programme.

#### Stage 2 — Preliminary Concept & Scoping (Gate 1: Idea Screen)
- Initial business case (rough BOM, target cost, target MSRP, target volume).
- Strategic fit check (does it align with brand? does it support EV roadmap?).
- **Output:** 8–15 qualified concepts advancing to scoping.

#### Stage 3 — Concept Development & Business Case (Gate 2: Second Screen)
- Detailed market research, segment sizing, competitor benchmarking.
- Target specifications (horsepower, range, boot space, infotainment screen size).
- Financial model: NPV, IRR, payback period, breakeven volume.
- **Output:** 2–4 fully scoped concepts.

#### Stage 4 — Product Design & Engineering (Gate 3: Go-to-Development)
- Styling freeze, full CAD/CAE, digital mock-up (DMU), virtual validation.
- Supplier nomination for long-lead items (semiconductors, battery cells).
- **Output:** Engineering Prototype (EP) builds.

#### Stage 5 — Prototype & Product Testing (Gate 4: Go-to-Validation)
- Alpha, Beta, and Gamma testing phases (detailed in Section 3).
- Durability, NVH, crash, homologation, EMC/EMI tests.
- **Output:** Pilot Production Build.

#### Stage 6 — Launch Readiness (Gate 5: Go-to-Launch)
- SOP (Start of Production), dealer readiness, service tooling, training.
- Marketing campaign, PR, influencer seeding.
- **Output:** Job #1 off the line, dealer demo cars dispatched.

#### Stage 7 — Post-Launch & Lifecycle Management (Gate 6: Post-Launch Review)
- 30/60/90-day quality audits, J.D. Power IQS, warranty cost tracking.
- Cost-down (VA/VE) exercises, facelift planning.
- **Output:** Continuous improvement pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks to "explain the NPD process", always draw or describe the **Stage-Gate model in chronological order** and explicitly mention the **decision gates**. This single move fetches 2–3 easy marks.

### 2.2 Market Research Techniques in Automotive NPD

Market research in the auto industry is split into **three distinct waves**, each with its own purpose, sample size, and budget.

| Wave | Purpose | Sample Size | Typical Tools | Automotive Example |
|------|---------|-------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Exploratory (Qualitative)** | Understand customer pain points, unmet needs | 30–80 | Focus groups, in-depth interviews, ethnography, ride-alongs, diary studies | Tata Motors shadowing Tier-2 city families to design the Punch |
| **Confirmatory (Quantitative)** | Quantify demand, willingness to pay, segment size | 1,000–10,000 | Online surveys, intercept surveys, concept tests, MaxDiff, Van Westendorp PSM | Online concept test for Hyundai Creta facelift with 5,000 respondents |
| **Validatory (Behavioural)** | Predict actual purchase behaviour | 5,000–50,000 | Conjoint analysis, choice modelling, simulator tests, test-drive surveys | Full-profile conjoint with 2,000 respondents to design Maruti Brezza variant lineup |

#### 2.2.1 Conjoint Analysis — The Heart of Automotive Market Research

**Conjoint analysis** is the *gold standard* quantitative technique used by every global OEM to determine how customers **trade off** competing attributes (price, fuel efficiency, boot space, ADAS features, brand).

A typical conjoint card presented to a respondent might be:

> *"Please choose which SUV you would buy:*
> - **Vehicle A:** Maruti, 1.5 L petrol, 18 kmpl, 380 L boot, 9-inch screen, sunroof, **₹14.5 Lakh**
> - **Vehicle B:** Hyundai, 1.5 L turbo petrol, 17 kmpl, 400 L boot, 10.25-inch screen, ADAS Level 1, **₹15.8 Lakh**"

From 8–12 such cards, a statistical model (**Hierarchical Bayes**) estimates **part-worth utilities** for each attribute level. These utilities are then aggregated to forecast market share using choice simulators.

#### 2.2.2 Voice of Customer (VoC) & Kano Model

The **Kano Model** (developed by Prof. Noriaki Kano, 1984) categorises product attributes into three buckets:

- **Must-Be (Basic) Attributes:** Absence causes dissatisfaction, presence is taken for granted. *Example:* Airbags, ABS, basic warranty.
- **One-Dimensional (Performance) Attributes:** Satisfaction is proportional to fulfilment. *Example:* Fuel efficiency, boot space, horsepower.
- **Attractive (Excitement) Attributes:** Unexpected delight that strongly differentiates. *Example:* Ventilated seats, 360° camera, V2L (Vehicle-to-Load) power output in EVs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Many students mistakenly believe customers always want "more features". The Kano model proves the opposite — *too many excitement features inflate cost without proportional satisfaction*. Smart OEMs convert excitement features into paid variants.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Automotive NPD & Market Research

| Concept | Formula / Model | Application in Auto NPD |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$$ | Determines whether a vehicle programme's projected cash flows justify the development investment. |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $$0 = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} - I_0$$ | Programme portfolio prioritisation; OEMs target **IRR > 18–22 %** for new programmes. |
| **Break-Even Volume (units)** | $$Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{(P - V)}$$ | Minimum units a new car must sell to cover fixed development + tooling cost. |
| **BCG Growth-Share Matrix** | Relative market share vs. market growth rate | Helps classify existing models as Star / Cash Cow / Question Mark / Dog during lifecycle review. |
| **Conjoint Utility Score** | $$U_{j} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_{k} \cdot x_{jk}$$ | Predicts preference for concept *j* by summing part-worths *β* across *K* attributes. |
| **Cannibalisation Rate** | $$Cannibalisation = \frac{Sales_{new,own}}{Sales_{new,new} + Sales_{old}} \times 100$$ | Measures how much a new variant (e.g., Brezza CNG) eats into the petrol variant's sales. |
| **Time-to-Market (TTM)** | $$TTM = M0 \rightarrow SOP \;(months)$$ | Strategic KPI; shorter TTM = first-mover pricing power. |
| **Warranty Cost Ratio (WCR)** | $$WCR = \frac{Warranty\ Claims}{Net\ Sales} \times 100$$ | Post-launch quality benchmark; OEMs target **WCR < 1.5 %**. |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $$NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$$ | Early-warning customer-loyalty metric tracked 30/60/90 days post-launch. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Warning:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` in the KTU table. Use `\vert` for absolute values, e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ as `$\vert x \vert$`. The Cooper Stage-Gate model is the single most important framework for this topic.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Frameworks

- **NPV / IRR** decide whether a low-volume sports car (Toyota GR86) deserves a green light even if it loses money in isolation but lifts brand equity.
- **Conjoint analysis** prevented Hyundai from over-investing in a digital key feature that buyers valued less than wireless Android Auto.
- **Kano classification** helped Mahindra prioritise ventilated seats over a panoramic sunroof in the XUV300, based on Indian climate pain points.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Concept Development & Product Testing

### 3.1 Concept Development — From Idea to Testable Concept

Concept development is the **single most creative and iterative sub-process** of automotive NPD. A "concept" is a **textual + visual + technical description** of the proposed vehicle that is *detailed enough to be tested with customers* but *not so detailed that it freezes engineering choices*.

#### Step-by-Step Process

**Step 1 — Concept Brief**
The marketing team, working with the Chief Design Officer, writes a one-page **Product Concept Brief (PCB)** that defines:
- Target segment (e.g., *"young urban professional couple, 28–35, Tier-1 city, first-time SUV upgrader"*).
- Positioning statement (e.g., *"India's most affordable Level-2 ADAS SUV"*).
- 3–5 **must-have** and 3–5 **delighter** features.

**Step 2 — Concept Generation**
Cross-functional teams (marketing, design, engineering, manufacturing, finance) generate 8–12 distinct concepts using:
- **Morphological analysis:** Combine attribute options (body style × powertrain × price × key feature).
- **Brand extension mapping:** What platform can be reused (e.g., Tata's ALFA-ARC platform shared between Punch and Nexon).
- **Benchmark teardowns:** Buy competitors' cars, dismantle them, log strengths/weaknesses.

**Step 3 — Concept Screening (Internal)**
Concepts are scored on **strategic fit, technical feasibility, financial viability, and brand fit** using a weighted scoring matrix. Only 2–4 concepts survive to customer testing.

**Step 4 — Concept Rendering & Specification**
For each surviving concept, the design team produces:
- **Exterior renderings** (2D sketches → 3D clay models).
- **Interior renderings** (dashboard layout, screen mockups).
- **Target spec sheet:** wheelbase, ground clearance, power output, range, boot space, infotainment, ADAS level.

**Step 5 — Concept Statement (The 3-Sentence Test)**
Each concept is condensed into a single **concept statement** following the classic template by **Geoffrey Moore** (Crossing the Chasm):

$$\text{For} \; \textit{(target customer)} \; \text{who} \; \textit{(statement of need / opportunity)},$$
$$\text{our} \; \textit{(product / category)} \; \text{is} \; \textit{(statement of key benefit)} \; \text{that} \; \textit{(reason to believe)}.$$

**Example (Tata Punch EV concept):**
> *"For the young, environmentally conscious Indian urbanite who wants to switch from a petrol hatchback to an EV but fears range anxiety, the Punch EV is a compact electric SUV that delivers a certified 350 km real-world range, fast-charging in under 60 minutes, and zero tailpipe emissions — at the price of a top-end petrol hatchback."*

### 3.2 Concept Testing — Validating Before Tooling Up

Concept testing is a **systematic, statistically valid survey** in which target customers evaluate one or more concept statements (often paired with visual renderings) and the marketer measures their purchase intent, perceived value, and uniqueness.

#### Step-by-Step Concept Test Process

**Step 1 — Define the Research Objective**
- *Example:* "Estimate purchase consideration for the proposed Tata Punch EV at ₹12.5 Lakh among current compact-SUV intenders."

**Step 2 — Choose the Research Design**
- **Monadic test:** Each respondent rates one concept (best for predicting single-product metrics).
- **Sequential monadic:** Each respondent rates multiple concepts in randomised order (best for **head-to-head** comparison).
- **Paired comparison / Choice-based conjoint:** Respondent picks preferred from pairs (best for trade-off data).

**Step 3 — Sample Design**
- **Screening questionnaire** filters for *category intenders* (people planning to buy an SUV in the next 12 months).
- Target sample: **n ≥ 400** for top-line consideration, **n ≥ 800** for sub-segment cuts.

**Step 4 — Stimulus Preparation**
- Concept card, 2–4 high-quality renderings, key spec bullets, indicative price.
- Avoid logos, brand names (use "Brand X") to prevent bias.

**Step 5 — Fieldwork & Data Collection**
- Online panel (e.g., Toluna, Dynata) + offline intercept at dealerships and auto expos.

**Step 6 — Analysis & Reporting**
- **Top-Box Purchase Intent (TBR):** % rating 4 or 5 on a 5-point scale.
- **Aided/Un-aided Awareness** delta.
- **Perceived value vs. price:** Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter.

#### Numerical Example — TBR Calculation

Suppose a concept test for an upcoming **Maruti compact SUV** yields the following on a 5-point purchase-intent scale (5 = Definitely would buy):

| Rating | % of Respondents |
|--------|------------------|
| 5 (Definitely) | 14 % |
| 4 (Probably) | 27 % |
| 3 (Maybe) | 31 % |
| 2 (Probably not) | 18 % |
| 1 (Definitely not) | 10 % |

**Top-Box Purchase Intent (TBR):**

$$TBR = \%(4) + \%(5) = 27\% + 14\% = 41\%$$

For a new compact SUV in the Indian market, a **TBR ≥ 35 %** is generally considered a **Go** signal, **25–35 %** is a **Recycle / Refine**, and **< 25 %** is typically a **Kill**.

**Average Purchase Intent (API):**

$$API = \frac{(5 \times 14) + (4 \times 27) + (3 \times 31) + (2 \times 18) + (1 \times 10)}{100}$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$API = \frac{70 + 108 + 93 + 36 + 10}{100} = \frac{317}{100} = 3.17$$

An **API between 3.2 and 3.6** is the industry benchmark for a healthy concept.

### 3.3 Product Testing — The Engineering Truth Serum

Once a concept survives, the engineering team builds prototypes and subjects them to a **structured, escalating test programme** known as the **A-B-C validation strategy** in automotive parlance.

#### A. Alpha Testing — Engineering Lab Validation

**Objective:** Validate that each sub-system meets its design specification.

- **Environment:** Climate chamber, NVH lab, shaker rig, engine dynamometer, EMC chamber.
- **Sample size:** 5–20 prototype units (often hand-built "mules").
- **Key tests:**
  - **Cold soak** at **−40 °C** (Himalayan winter, trans-Siberian transit).
  - **Hot soak** at **+55 °C**, 95 % humidity (Rajasthan summer, monsoon Mumbai).
  - **Dust ingress** (IP6X validation).
  - **Endurance:** 24/7 dyno runs for **2,000–5,000 hours** equivalent to **150,000 km**.
- **Gate decision:** Go to Beta only if **DFMEA (Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** risk scores fall below threshold.

#### B. Beta Testing — Real-World Customer Pilot

**Objective:** Validate the vehicle in **real customer hands, on real roads, in real weather**.

- **Sample size:** 50–200 units.
- **Participants:** Internal employees, friendly fleet customers, dealer principals, R&D partner families.
- **Duration:** 3–9 months, covering **20,000–50,000 km** per vehicle.
- **Test routes:** Mandatory coverage of **high-altitude (Leh), desert (Jaisalmer), coastal humidity (Chennai), congested city (Mumbai), and high-speed expressway (NE-1)**.
- **Data captured:** Daily driver diary, CAN-bus logs, OBD-II fault codes, photo evidence of any issue.
- **Hallmark of a successful Beta:** **Zero Cat-A (safety) issues** and **< 5 Cat-B (comfort) issues** per 1,000 km.

#### C. Gamma Testing — Pre-Production Pilot Build Validation

**Objective:** Validate the **manufacturing process**, supplier quality, and dealership readiness.

- **Sample size:** 100–500 units built on the **actual production line** using **production tooling**.
- **Customer handovers:** Usually not yet sold to retail; used as **press fleet, dealer demos, and internal company cars**.
- **Key validations:**
  - First Pass Yield (FPY) at every station.
  - Paint shop adhesion, BIW (Body-in-White) dimensional audit, torque audit.
  - **J.D. Power IQS proxy** simulation — drive 100 km and inspect for **squeaks, rattles, misalignments, water leaks**.
- **Output:** The **Job #1 SOP (Start of Production)** milestone is declared when Gamma meets all quality, cost, and timing targets.

#### Worked Numerical Example — Failure Rate Calculation in Beta

A Beta fleet of **120 vehicles** accumulates a total of **1,440,000 km** in 6 months. The team logs **864 total warranty-classified issues**, of which **72 are Cat-A (safety-critical)**.

**Issue density (overall):**

$$ID_{overall} = \frac{864 \; issues}{1,440,000 \; km} = 0.0006 \; issues/km$$

Expressed as **Issues per 1,000 km (IPTV — Issues Per Thousand Vehicles / km):**

$$IPTV = 0.0006 \times 1000 = 0.60 \; issues/1000\,km$$

**Cat-A density:**

$$ID_{Cat-A} = \frac{72}{1,440,000} = 0.00005 \; issues/km = 0.05 \; per\;1000\,km$$

**Interpretation:** A typical automotive target at Beta sign-off is **< 0.5 IPTV overall** and **< 0.05 IPTV Cat-A**. The fleet is therefore *just at threshold for Cat-A* and *slightly above threshold for overall issues* — the **Programme Steering Committee should Recycle** the build for an additional engineering fix loop rather than proceeding straight to Gamma.

### 3.4 Stage-Gate Deliverable Checklist (Examiner's Favourite Table)

| Gate | Decision | Required Deliverables | Approx. Cost Spent (Cumulative) |
|------|----------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 0 — Discovery | Proceed to ideation | Strategic plan, market trends | < 1 % |
| 1 — Idea Screen | Kill / Recycle / Continue | Screened idea pool, rough business case | ~2 % |
| 2 — Second Screen | Choose 1–3 concepts | Full business case, market research, target specs | ~7 % |
| 3 — Go to Development | Authorise engineering | Frozen styling, CAD, supplier RFQ | ~20 % |
| 4 — Go to Validation | Authorise tooling | Alpha + Beta test reports, regulatory sign-off | ~55 % |
| 5 — Go to Launch | Authorise SOP | Marketing plan, dealer readiness, J.D. Power targets | ~85 % |
| 6 — Post-Launch Review | Close programme | NPS, IQS, warranty cost actuals | 100 % |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Stage-Gate Process for Automotive NPD

<!-- MERMAID_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Stage 1: Idea Generation]:::stage --> G1{Gate 1: Idea Screen}:::gate
    G1 -- Kill --> K1[Discard]:::kill
    G1 -- Recycle --> A0
    G1 -- Pass --> A1[Stage 2: Scoping and Preliminary Concept]:::stage
    A1 --> G2{Gate 2: Second Screen}:::gate
    G2 -- Kill --> K1
    G2 -- Pass --> A2[Stage 3: Concept Development and Business Case]:::stage
    A2 --> G3{Gate 3: Go to Development}:::gate
    G3 -- Pass --> A3[Stage 4: Product Design and Engineering CAD CAE]:::stage
    A3 --> G4{Gate 4: Go to Validation}:::gate
    G4 -- Pass --> A4[Stage 5: Alpha Beta Gamma Product Testing]:::stage
    A4 --> G5{Gate 5: Go to Launch}:::gate
    G5 -- Pass --> A5[Stage 6: Launch and Start of Production]:::stage
    A5 --> G6{Gate 6: Post Launch Review}:::gate
    G6 --> A6[Stage 7: Lifecycle Costdown and Facelift]:::stage
    A6 --> A0

    classDef gate fill:#C00000,stroke:#7F0000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef kill fill:#7F7F7F,stroke:#404040,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:1px
```
