## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC). Mention any two key benefits of IMC in the automotive industry.**

**Model Answer:**

Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) is a strategic business process used to plan, develop, execute, and evaluate coordinated, measurable, persuasive brand communication programs over time with consumers, customers, prospects, and other relevant audiences, to generate both short-term sales and long-term brand equity (American Association of Advertising Agencies).

**Two Key Benefits in Automotive Industry:**
1. **Consistent Brand Message** – A buyer sees the same Hyundai Venue value proposition (smart, bold, tech-loaded) across TV, Instagram, and the dealership, reinforcing recall.
2. **Cost Efficiency** – Coordinated campaign reduces duplication; e.g., a digital video can be repurposed for TV, social media, and dealer screens, saving production costs.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Advertising and Public Relations. List three tools of sales promotion used by car manufacturers.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Advertising | Public Relations |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Paid by the sponsor | Mostly free/earned media |
| Control | Full control over message | Limited control (media decides coverage) |
| Credibility | Moderate (audience knows it is paid) | High (third-party endorsement) |
| Purpose | Persuade to buy | Build goodwill and reputation |

**Three Sales Promotion Tools Used by Car Manufacturers:**
1. **Cashback Offers** – e.g., ₹50,000 cashback during festive season on Tata Nexon.
2. **Free Accessories Bundles** – e.g., free dashcam and floor mats with Hyundai Creta bookings.
3. **Low-Interest Finance Schemes** – e.g., 6.99% p.a. car loan via manufacturer tie-up with HDFC Bank.

---

### PART B — Essay / Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] [14 Marks]**
>
> **QUESTION A:**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC). Describe the IMC planning process in detail with reference to the automotive industry.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the role of advertising as a marketing communication tool in the Indian automotive sector. Illustrate with two real-life brand campaigns.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Concept of IMC and Planning Process:**

Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) is a customer-centric, data-driven, results-oriented strategic business process that integrates and coordinates all communication channels—advertising, sales promotion, public relations, direct marketing, digital marketing, and personal selling—to deliver a unified, consistent, and compelling brand message.

According to **Philip Kotler and Kevin Lane Keller**, IMC improves marketing communication efficiency by combining all tools in a cohesive manner. According to **Schultz, Tannenbaum, and Lauterborn**, IMC views the customer as the central focus and starts from the *customer back* rather than from the brand forward.

**The IMC Planning Process (applied to automotive industry):**

**Step 1: Identify Target Customers**
The car manufacturer identifies segments using demographic (age, income), geographic (Tier-1/2 cities), psychographic (aspirational, status-conscious), and behavioural (online researcher, family buyer) data. Example: Tata Motors identified that the **Nexon EV** targets environmentally-conscious urban professionals aged 28–40 with monthly income above ₹80,000.

**Step 2: Set Communication Objectives**
Following the **DAGMAR model**, objectives move from awareness → comprehension → conviction → action. For a new launch like the **Mahindra XUV 3XO**, objectives might be: achieve 70% awareness in 60 days, 30% consideration lift, and 15,000 bookings.

**Step 3: Design the Message and Select Channels**
The brand message is crafted (e.g., "XUV 3XO – Born to Roar"). Channels are selected: TV (mass awareness), YouTube (engagement), Instagram (aspiration), dealer events (trial), email (retargeting). The **Message Mix** balances rational (mileage, safety rating) and emotional (pride, family bonding) appeals.

**Step 4: Execute the Campaign**
Coordinated rollout across all channels with synchronized timing. Example: Mahindra coordinated the XUV 3XO launch across a single weekend — TV premiere, digital reveal, dealer launch event, and influencer reviews dropped simultaneously.

**Step 5: Measure and Refine**
KPIs tracked include aided/unaided awareness, social media engagement, footfalls, test drives, bookings, and ultimately sales conversion. Data is fed back into the next campaign cycle.

[7 Marks = 1 mark concept + 2 marks process steps + 1 mark DAGMAR/AIDA + 1 mark automotive example + 2 marks application]

**(b) Role of Advertising in Indian Automotive Sector — Two Real-Life Campaigns:**

Advertising is the most visible and dominant component of the IMC mix for automobiles. Indian carmakers spent over **₹6,000 crore** on advertising in FY24. Advertising builds awareness, shapes perception, differentiates the brand, supports dealers, and drives sales.

**Campaign 1: Maruti Suzuki — "Be Heard" (Verna Campaign)**
Maruti Suzuki targeted young professionals with the **Verna sedan** through the "Be Heard" campaign. The TVC featured urban youth driving through city streets, with the message of self-expression. The campaign blended emotional appeal (individuality) with rational appeal (tech-loaded features like sunroof, ADAS, ventilated seats). It aired on TV, YouTube, Instagram, and Spotify, generating 50 million+ digital impressions and 18% lift in showroom visits.

**Campaign 2: Tata Motors — "Connecting Aspirations" (Tiago Campaign)**
Tata Motors' Tiago campaign focused on first-time car buyers seeking safety and style at an affordable price. The campaign highlighted the **5-star GNCAP safety rating** — a strong rational appeal — combined with the emotional appeal of family pride. The TVC was supported by digital short films on Tata's YouTube channel and dealer-level activation.

**Strategic Role of Advertising:**
1. **Mass Awareness** – TV reach of 80%+ in target markets.
2. **Brand Image Building** – Luxury brands (BMW, Audi) maintain premium positioning via aspirational ads.
3. **Product Differentiation** – "India's safest car" claim for Tata Punch.
4. **Dealer Support** – National ads drive footfall to local showrooms.
5. **Sales Conversion** – Effective ads shorten the buyer decision cycle.

[7 Marks = 2 marks role overview + 2 marks campaign 1 detail + 2 marks campaign 2 detail + 1 mark strategic conclusions]

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024] [14 Marks]**
>
> **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the major tools of sales promotion used in the automotive industry. Differentiate between consumer-oriented and trade-oriented sales promotions with examples.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Analyze the growing importance of digital marketing in the Indian automotive sector. Discuss at least four digital marketing tools with suitable examples.

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Sales Promotion Tools in Automotive Industry:**

Sales promotion comprises short-term incentives designed to stimulate quicker or greater purchase of a product. In the highly competitive Indian car market, sales promotions play a vital role in clearing inventory, countering competitor offers, and accelerating buyer decisions.

**Consumer-Oriented Sales Promotions** — Targeted at the **end buyer**:
1. **Cash Discounts & Price-Offs** – Flat ₹50,000 off on Hyundai Creta during festive season.
2. **Cashback Offers** – ₹25,000 cashback on EMI transactions with HDFC Bank credit cards.
3. **Free Accessories** – Bundled seat covers, floor mats, reverse parking camera.
4. **Scratch Cards / Lucky Draws** – "Win a free Goa trip with every test drive" campaign by Kia.
5. **Extended Warranty** – Free 5-year warranty instead of standard 3-year.
6. **Low-Interest Finance** – 6.99% p.a. tie-up with manufacturer finance arm.
7. **Corporate Discounts** – Special price for IT/PSU employees (e.g., Tata Motors' corporate scheme).

**Trade-Oriented Sales Promotions** — Targeted at **dealers and channel partners**:
1. **Dealer Margins** – Higher per-unit margin during slow months to incentivize push.
2. **Volume Discounts** – Cumulative bonus for dealers crossing sales targets.
3. **Display Allowances** – Reimbursement for dedicated showroom space or new display boards.
4. **Salesperson Contests** – Free international trip (e.g., Seoul) for top performers.
5. **Co-operative Advertising** – Manufacturer funds a portion of dealer's local newspaper ad.
6. **Inventory Financing Support** – Low-interest credit for dealers to stock more vehicles.
7. **Training & Certification Bonuses** – Dealers get extra margin for certified sales staff.

**Key Differences:**

| Basis | Consumer-Oriented | Trade-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| Target | End buyer | Dealer / Channel partner |
| Goal | Stimulate trial/purchase | Ensure push, motivation, stocking |
| Examples | Cashback, EMI offers | Dealer margins, contests |
| Duration | Short-term, often seasonal | Quarterly/annual schemes |
| Spend Source | Manufacturer's brand budget | Channel/trade marketing budget |

[7 Marks = 2 marks definition + 2 marks consumer tools + 2 marks trade tools + 1 mark differentiation table]

**(b) Growing Importance of Digital Marketing in Indian Automotive Sector:**

India's internet penetration has crossed **950 million users** as of 2024, and **over 78% of car buyers research online** before visiting a dealership (Google Auto Insights, 2023). Digital marketing is no longer optional — it is the *centrepiece* of automotive IMC.

**Reasons for Digital Marketing's Growth:**
1. **Buyer Journey is Now Digital-First** – Buyers compare models, read reviews, watch YouTube videos before deciding.
2. **Precision Targeting** – Meta, Google, and YouTube allow targeting by income, interest, and intent.
3. **Measurable ROI** – Every rupee spent is trackable via analytics.
4. **Cost Efficiency** – A digital video can reach millions at a fraction of TV cost.
5. **Two-Way Engagement** – Brands can reply, engage, and build community (vs. one-way TV).
6. **Personalization** – Ads tailored to a user's previous browsing of "compact SUV".

**Four Key Digital Marketing Tools:**

**1. Social Media Marketing (Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, LinkedIn):**
Brands post reels, stories, and short videos showing car features, lifestyle, and customer testimonials. Example: **MG Motor India** runs extensive Instagram campaigns with cinematic reels of the Hector and Astor, often featuring Bollywood actor Nitin Gadkari-era brand ambassadors. Tata Motors' Nexon EV leverages LinkedIn for corporate fleet buyers and Instagram for end consumers.

**2. Search Engine Marketing (Google Ads, SEO):**
When a user searches "best compact SUV under 15 lakhs", brands bid for visibility. Example: **Hyundai Motor India** runs Google Ads campaigns targeting keywords like "Creta on-road price Mumbai" with location-specific landing pages. SEO-optimized blogs on "Creta vs. Seltos comparison" appear on top of organic search results.

**3. Influencer Marketing:**
Auto vloggers and lifestyle influencers are paid for honest reviews and lifestyle placement. Example: **PowerDrift, Beebom, and Tech Burner** on YouTube review new launches like Tata Punch EV. Lifestyle influencers showcase Mahindra Thar in adventure content.

**4. Content Marketing and Online Configurators:**
Brand websites host blogs, comparison tools, AR/VR configurators, and video galleries. Example: **Hyundai Click-to-Buy** platform allows end-to-end online purchase with home delivery. Tata Motors' AR configurator lets buyers place a virtual Nexon in their driveway using smartphone camera.

**Bonus Tools (Brief Mention):**
- **Email Marketing** – Lead nurturing via drip campaigns.
- **Chatbots & AI** – 24/7 customer query resolution (Tata Motors' EV chatbot).
- **Programmatic Advertising** – Real-time bidding across websites.
- **Affiliate Marketing** – Tie-ups with auto portals like CarWale, CarDekho.

[7 Marks = 1 mark context + 2 marks reasons + 4 marks (1 per digital tool) ]

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in IMC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Defining IMC without quoting authority** — Always cite Philip Kotler, AAAA, or Schultz. ❌ *Loss: 1 mark per question*
>
> 2. **Listing tools without industry examples** — Every concept MUST be tied to a real automotive brand (Tata, Hyundai, Maruti, MG, Mahindra). ❌ *Loss: 2 marks*
>
> 3. **Confusing Advertising with Marketing** — Advertising is ONE tool; Marketing is the broader discipline. ❌ *Loss: 1–2 marks*
>
> 4. **Ignoring the "Integration" in IMC** — Simply listing the 4 tools without showing how they work together is incomplete. Students must emphasize *coordination and consistency*. ❌ *Loss: 2–3 marks*
>
> 5. **No numerical example** — When asked about metrics, students skip formulas like GRP, CPM, ROMI. Always include one calculation. ❌ *Loss: 2 marks*
>
> 6. **Skipping DAGMAR/AIDA models** — These are examiner favourites. Always mention them in any advertising/IMC answer. ❌ *Loss: 1–2 marks*
>
> 7. **Generic answers without India context** — KTU examiners prefer India-specific examples (Indian brands, Indian festivals, Indian buyers). Use Maruti/Tata/Hyundai over BMW/Toyota wherever possible.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Summary — Print This Before Exam:**

- [x] **IMC Definition** — Coordinated, measurable brand communication across all channels (AAAA Definition).
- [x] **4 Major Tools** — Advertising, Public Relations, Sales Promotion, Digital Marketing.
- [x] **Advertising** — Paid, non-personal, mass media; tools include TV, Print, OOH, Radio, Cinema.
- [x] **Public Relations** — Earned media; press releases, events, sponsorships, crisis management.
- [x] **Sales Promotion** — Short-term incentives; consumer-oriented (cashback) and trade-oriented (dealer margins).
- [x] **Digital Marketing** — SEO, SEM, SMM, Content, Email, Influencer, Mobile, Programmatic.
- [x] **DAGMAR Model** — Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action.
- [x] **AIDA Model** — Attention → Interest → Desire → Action.
- [x] **Schultz IMC 4 Steps** — Identify Audience → Objectives → Message/Channels → Measure.
- [x] **Reach** = Unique individuals exposed ÷ Target population × 100.
- [x] **Frequency** = Total exposures ÷ Reach.
- [x] **GRP** = Reach × Frequency.
- [x] **CPM** = (Cost × 1000) ÷ Impressions.
- [x] **ROMI** = (Sales Lift − Marketing Cost) ÷ Marketing Cost.
- [x] **CAC** = Marketing Spend ÷ New Customers.
- [x] **NPS** = %Promoters − %Detractors.
- [x] **CTR** = (Clicks ÷ Impressions) × 100.
- [x] **Conversion Rate** = (Conversions ÷ Visitors) × 100.
- [x] **SOV** = Brand's ad spend ÷ Category ad spend × 100.
- [x] **Typical IMC Budget Split** — Advertising 40%, Digital 25%, Sales Promo 20%, PR 8%, Others 7%.
- [x] **5 Customer Journey Stages** — Need Recognition → Search → Evaluation → Purchase → Post-Purchase.
- [x] **Real Indian Brand Examples** — Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, Mahindra, MG, Kia, Toyota, BMW.
- [x] **Digital Dominance** — 78% of Indian car buyers research online; YouTube is the #1 research platform.
- [x] **Crisis PR** — Critical for recalls, scandals, executive exits.
- [x] **Influencer Marketing** — Auto vloggers (PowerDrift, Beebom, Tech Burner) command millions of viewers.
- [x] **Always use India-specific examples** in KTU exams for full marks.
- [x] **Always cite at least one authority** (Kotler, AAAA, Belch, Schultz) in definition-type answers.

topics.title: Advertising and Media Planning - Planning and Executing Advertising Campaigns for Automotive Products, Including Media Selection, Message Development and Campaign Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advertising and Media Planning - Planning and Executing Advertising Campaigns for Automotive Products, Including Media Selection, Message Development and Campaign Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Advertising Campaign – Formal Academic Definition

An **advertising campaign** is a series of coordinated, strategically linked promotional messages and media placements designed and executed over a specified period to communicate a unified brand proposition, influence consumer attitudes, and achieve measurable marketing objectives for a specific product, service, or organization (Kotler & Keller, *Marketing Management* framework). For **automotive products**, an advertising campaign is the structured, multi-channel communication effort undertaken by an Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) or dealer to promote a specific vehicle, model range, brand, or value-added service across pre-selected media vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Framing):**
> Advertising is any paid form of non-personal presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor. **Media Planning** is the series of decisions involved in delivering the promotional message to the prospective consumer in the right place, at the right time, and at the lowest possible cost.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy – The "Movie Trailer" Model

Imagine an automotive launch is a feature film, and the **advertising campaign** is the *trailer campaign* the studio runs before the release. The trailer must:

- **Capture the essence** of the movie in 90 seconds → *Message Development*.
- **Run on YouTube, TV, Instagram Reels, cinema halls, and billboards in malls** → *Media Selection*.
- **Appear at the right frequency** before the release date → *Scheduling & Reach*.
- **Be reviewed by the audience** (likes, shares, footfalls in dealerships) → *Campaign Evaluation*.

Just as a movie trailer that runs in the wrong theater or with the wrong tone can kill a film's opening weekend, an automotive campaign that selects the wrong media (e.g., Facebook for senior luxury buyers) or sends the wrong message (e.g., fuel-economy claims for a sports car) will fail to generate showroom visits.

## 1.3 The Automotive Industry Context

Automotive advertising is uniquely complex because:

| Factor | Description |
|---|---|
| **High Involvement** | Cars are high-cost, high-risk purchases → need detailed information |
| **Long Decision Cycle** | 3–9 months from awareness to purchase |
| **Emotional + Rational Mix** | Buyers respond to both aspirational imagery and technical specs |
| **Globalized Brands** | Campaigns must adapt to local culture (e.g., Maruti vs. BMW India) |
| **Regulatory Environment** | ASCI (Advertising Standards Council of India) guidelines, FAME-II norms for EVs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Automotive Industry Metric – CPCV (Cost Per Qualified Vehicle Lead):**
> A key performance metric specific to the auto industry measuring the cost of acquiring a verified potential buyer (test-drive booked, brochure downloaded, etc.) through an advertising campaign. Typical benchmark: **₹800 – ₹2,500 per lead** in the Indian mass-market segment.

## 1.4 Media Planning – Core Definition

**Media Planning** is the process of determining **which media vehicles** (specific TV channels, newspapers, websites, radio stations, billboards) to use, **when** to use them, **how often**, and **in what combination** to deliver the advertising message to the target audience effectively and economically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Ms of Advertising (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> 1. **Mission** – Advertising objectives
> 2. **Money** – Budget allocation
> 3. **Message** – Creative content
> 4. **Media** – Channel selection
> 5. **Measurement** – Evaluation of outcomes

## 1.5 Section 1 Visual Snapshot

> **Automotive Campaign = Strategy × Creative × Media × Evaluation**
> A campaign fails not because the car is bad, but because the **message-media fit** is broken.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Advertising Campaign Planning Process (AIDA-Enhanced Framework)

A successful automotive advertising campaign follows a sequential decision framework, often mapped to the **AIDA Model** (Attention → Interest → Desire → Action) but operationalized through five distinct managerial stages:

### Stage 1: Identify Target Audience
- Demographics: Age (e.g., 28–35 for compact SUVs), Income (₹8L–₹15L PA), Occupation
- Psychographics: Lifestyle (aspirational, family-oriented, status-driven)
- Behavioral: Current vehicle ownership, online search patterns, test-drive history
- **Auto-Specific Layer:** *Buying role* – Influencer (spouse), Decider (head of family), User (daily driver), Purchaser (financier)

### Stage 2: Set Advertising Objectives (DAGMAR Framework)

The **DAGMAR Model** (Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results) requires objectives to be:
- **Specific** (e.g., "Increase brand recall by 15%")
- **Measurable** (quantifiable metric)
- **Achievable** (realistic given budget)
- **Relevant** (tied to business goal)

$$ \text{Advertising Objective} = f(\text{Awareness}, \text{Attitude}, \text{Intent}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication vs. Sales Objectives:**
> - **Communication objectives** (intermediate): Awareness, recall, comprehension, persuasion
> - **Sales objectives** (ultimate): Unit sales, market share, revenue
> KTU examiners consistently test the distinction – a campaign that boosts awareness may not boost sales.

### Stage 3: Establish the Advertising Budget

Four classical methods (KTU favourites):

| Method | Formula / Logic | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Percentage-of-Sales** | Budget = X% × Last Year's Sales | Maruti allocates 4% of revenue |
| **Objective-and-Task** | Sum of costs of tasks needed | Calculate cost of 1000 GRPs × rate |
| **Competitive Parity** | Match competitor's ad spend | Match Hyundai's outlay |
| **Affordable** | Whatever is left over | Risk: underfunding |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Percentage-of-Sales Flaw:** Penalizes successful brands with growing sales and rewards declining ones. Often criticized in KTU case discussions.

### Stage 4: Develop Advertising Message (Message Strategy)

Two classical creative approaches:

$$ \text{Message Strategy} = \begin{cases} \text{Informational (rational)} \rightarrow \text{Feature-benefit copy} \\ \text{Emotional (transformational)} \rightarrow \text{Aspirational imagery} \end{cases} $$

**Automotive Context:** Luxury brands (BMW, Mercedes) lean emotional. Mass-market brands (Maruti, Hyundai) blend both. EV campaigns lean informational (range, charging time) but aspirational (sustainability).

### Stage 5: Select Media & Evaluate Campaign

This is the heart of **Media Planning** – covered in depth below.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Application in Auto Campaigns |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reach (R)** | $\% \text{ of target audience exposed at least once}$ | "Reach 70% of SUV intenders in South India" |
| 2 | **Frequency (F)** | $\text{Average exposures per reached person}$ | "3 exposures per prospect in 30 days" |
| 3 | **Gross Rating Points (GRPs)** | $GRP = R \times F$ | Total weight of media buy |
| 4 | **Target Rating Points (TRPs)** | $\text{TRP} = \text{GRPs} \times \frac{\text{Target Audience \%}}{\text{Total Population \%}}$ | True efficiency for niche audience |
| 5 | **Cost Per Thousand (CPM)** | $CPM = \frac{\text{Cost of ad slot}}{\text{Reach} \div 1000}$ | Compare TV vs. YouTube efficiency |
| 6 | **Cost Per Point (CPP)** | $CPP = \frac{\text{Cost of schedule}}{\text{GRPs delivered}}$ | Negotiate with broadcasters |
| 7 | **Effective Reach** | $\% \text{ exposed at minimum effective frequency (3+)}$ | Industry-standard ≥ 3 exposures |
| 8 | **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $SOV = \frac{\text{Brand's Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Competitive tracking |
| 9 | **Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)** | $ROAS = \frac{\text{Incremental Revenue}}{\text{Ad Spend}}$ | Profitability check |
| 10 | **CPCV (Cost Per Qualified Vehicle Lead)** | $CPCV = \frac{\text{Total Campaign Cost}}{\text{Verified Leads}}$ | Auto-specific KPI |
| 11 | **VTR (View-Through Rate)** | $VTR = \frac{\text{Views of full ad}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | YouTube/Instagram Reels |
| 12 | **Ad Recall Score** | $\frac{\text{Recalled ad mentions}}{\text{Total surveyed}} \times 100$ | Post-campaign research |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Frequently Tested:** Calculate GRPs and CPM. Example: A Maruti campaign costs ₹50,00,000 to reach 2 crore people. Then:
> $$ CPM = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \div 1000} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹250 \text{ per thousand} $$

## 2.3 Media Selection Framework – Detailed

### The Three Core Decisions

**Decision A: Reach vs. Frequency**

$$ \text{Optimal Mix} = f(\text{Product Complexity}, \text{Competition Intensity}, \text{Stage in PLC}) $$

- **New model launch** → High reach, moderate frequency
- **Mature model in cluttered market** → High frequency on loyal segment
- **EV with new tech** → High frequency to educate

**Decision B: Continuous vs. Pulsing vs. Flighting**

| Pattern | Description | Auto Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous** | Steady ad spend throughout year | Brand-building for established model |
| **Flighting** | Heavy bursts interspersed with silence | Seasonal sales (festive, year-end) |
| **Pulsing** | Continuous low spend + periodic bursts | Balanced – preferred for auto |
| **Massed** | All spend in one concentrated burst | Limited edition launch |

**Decision C: Media Mix Allocation**

The classic allocation problem:

$$ \min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i x_i \geq R_{\text{target}} $$

Where:
- $C_i$ = cost of media vehicle $i$
- $x_i$ = number of insertions in vehicle $i$
- $R_i$ = reach contribution of vehicle $i$
- $R_{\text{target}}$ = minimum required reach

## 2.4 Media Vehicle Suitability for Automotive

| Media | Strength | Weakness | Auto Fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Television** | Mass reach, emotional storytelling, audio-visual impact | High cost, declining viewership, ad-skipping | Excellent for launches |
| **Print (Newspapers)** | Localized, credible, detailed specs | Slow, declining readership | Dealer-level promos |
| **Radio** | In-car listening reach, low cost | Audio-only, fleeting | City-specific offers |
| **Outdoor (OOH)** | Highway billboards, city presence | Static, no detail | Brand reminders near dealerships |
| **Digital Display** | Programmatic targeting | Banner blindness | Remarketing to site visitors |
| **YouTube / OTT** | Long-form storytelling, skip-able | Ad-skipping | Aspirational video content |
| **Social Media (IG/FB)** | Engagement, UGC, influencer tie-ups | Algorithm volatility | Lifestyle imagery, reels |
| **Search (Google Ads)** | Captures active intent | Cluttered, expensive keywords | "Best SUV under 15 lakh" |
| **Auto Portals (CarWale, CarDekho)** | High purchase intent | Limited reach | Listings + display |
| **Cinema** | Captive audience, premium feel | High cost, niche | Luxury model launches |

## 2.5 Message Development – The Creative Brief

A creative brief is the single-page document that bridges strategy and execution:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Framework: The 7 Elements of a Creative Brief (Automotive Variant)**
> 1. **Objective** – What should the ad accomplish?
> 2. **Target Audience** – *Primary*: e.g., "Tech-savvy first-time SUV buyer, 30–38, Bangalore"
> 3. **Competitor Frame** – What are we differentiating from?
> 4. **Consumer Insight** – "Wants weekend adventure but fears sedan practicality"
> 5. **Key Benefit / Promise** – *"Adventure-ready, city-smart"*
> 6. **Support / Reason-to-Believe** – *Ground clearance 210mm, 5-star GNCAP rating*
> 7. **Tone & Mandatories** – *Tone: confident, warm. Mandatories: logo, price range, fuel type*

## 2.6 Theoretical Foundations: Message Strategy Models

### The Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM)
- **Central route** → Logical arguments (technical specs, mileage) for high-involvement buyers
- **Peripheral route** → Cues like celebrity, music, attractive visuals (low-involvement/impulse)

### The FCB Grid (Foote, Cone & Belding)
Maps **thinking vs. feeling** buyers against **high vs. low involvement** to determine whether the ad should emphasize **information** or **emotion**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students write that automotive ads are always emotional. In reality, **fleet buyers and commercial vehicles** (e.g., Tata Ace, Ashok Leyland trucks) are pure information-processing (high-think, high-involve).

## 2.7 Campaign Evaluation Framework

**Pre-testing** (before launch):
- Concept testing, copy testing, storyboard tests
- Methods: focus groups, in-depth interviews, persuasion scores

**Post-testing** (after launch):
- **Communication effects:** Recall, recognition, attitude change
- **Sales effects:** Sales lift, market share, inquiry generation
- **Methods:** A/B testing, geo-experiments, brand tracking studies

$$ \text{Effectiveness Index} = \frac{\text{Actual Sales Lift}}{\text{Expected Sales Lift}} \times 100 $$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Analytical Problem 1: GRP and Reach-Frequency Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> Maruti Suzuki launches the new Swift with a 3-week pre-launch campaign. The media plan delivers ads to **8 million people** in the target segment. The campaign's frequency is set at **4 exposures per reached person**. Calculate the Gross Rating Points (GRPs) if the target universe is **10 million**.

### Step 1: Define Given Variables

$$ \text{Reach} = 8{,}000{,}000 \quad \text{people} $$
$$ \text{Frequency} = 4 \quad \text{exposures per person} $$
$$ \text{Target Universe} = 10{,}000{,}000 \quad \text{people} $$

### Step 2: Calculate Reach as a Percentage

$$ R_{\%} = \frac{\text{Reach}}{\text{Target Universe}} \times 100 $$

$$ R_{\%} = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 80\% $$

### Step 3: Apply the GRP Formula

$$ GRP = R_{\%} \times F $$

$$ GRP = 80 \times 4 = 320 \text{ GRPs} $$

### Step 4: Interpretation

A GRP of 320 means the campaign delivers the **equivalent of 320% of the target universe in exposures** over the campaign period. This is a **heavy-weight campaign**, suitable for a major launch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always express Reach as a percentage (not a number) before multiplying by frequency. This is the most common mistake.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Problem 2: CPM Comparison Across Media

> **Problem Statement:**
> A Hyundai Creta campaign evaluates two media options:
> - **Option A (TV):** Cost per spot = ₹3,00,000; Spot reaches 5,00,000 viewers
> - **Option B (YouTube):** Cost per 30-sec ad = ₹2,00,000; Ad reaches 8,00,000 impressions
>
> Which medium offers better cost efficiency?

### Step 1: CPM for TV (Option A)

$$ CPM_{TV} = \frac{\text{Cost}}{\text{Reach} \div 1000} $$

$$ CPM_{TV} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000 \div 1000} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{500} = ₹600 $$

### Step 2: CPM for YouTube (Option B)

$$ CPM_{YT} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000 \div 1000} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{800} = ₹250 $$

### Step 3: Comparative Analysis

$$ \text{Savings per 1000} = 600 - 250 = ₹350 $$

$$ \text{Savings Ratio} = \frac{600 - 250}{600} \times 100 = 58.33\% $$

### Step 4: Managerial Conclusion

YouTube delivers a **58.33% lower cost per thousand reach** than TV. However, the decision must also factor in:
- **Engagement quality** (TV emotional impact vs. YouTube skip rate)
- **Audience fit** (Hyundai Creta's target is mass-market, family SUV)
- **Brand-building role** (TV has superior mass-brand equity impact)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Lower CPM is NOT always the best choice. A medium must also satisfy the *qualitative* criteria of audience relevance and creative compatibility.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Problem 3: ROAS Calculation

> **Problem Statement:**
> Tata Motors spends ₹2 crore on a Nexon EV campaign. The campaign generates:
> - 5,000 qualified leads
> - 8% conversion rate to sales
> - Average revenue per vehicle: ₹15 lakh
>
> Calculate the ROAS and CPCV.

### Step 1: Calculate Number of Vehicles Sold

$$ \text{Sales} = \text{Leads} \times \text{Conversion Rate} $$

$$ \text{Sales} = 5{,}000 \times 0.08 = 400 \text{ vehicles} $$

### Step 2: Calculate Total Revenue

$$ \text{Revenue} = 400 \times ₹15{,}00{,}000 = ₹60{,}00{,}00{,}000 $$

### Step 3: Calculate ROAS

$$ ROAS = \frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Ad Spend}} $$

$$ ROAS = \frac{60{,}00{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 30:1 $$

> **Interpretation:** For every ₹1 spent on advertising, ₹30 in revenue is generated. Industry benchmark for auto is 10:1 to 25:1, so 30:1 is excellent.

### Step 4: Calculate CPCV

$$ CPCV = \frac{\text{Ad Spend}}{\text{Verified Leads}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = ₹4{,}000 \text{ per lead} $$

> This is slightly above the mass-market benchmark (₹800–₹2,500) but acceptable for an **EV (early-adopter segment)** with higher consideration costs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Media Selection Decision Tree for Automotive

| Brand Stage | Primary Objective | Recommended Media | Frequency | Creative Tone |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **New Model Launch** | Awareness | TV + YouTube + Outdoor + Auto Portals | High (5–7) | Aspirational |
| **Established Model – Sales Push** | Lead Generation | Search + Social + Auto Portals | Medium (3) | Offer-driven |
| **Mature Model – Loyalty** | Retention | Email + Drip Marketing + CRM | Low (1–2) | Service-oriented |
| **EV / Tech-heavy Model** | Education | YouTube Long-form + Influencer + Auto Reviews | High (4–6) | Informational + Aspirational |
| **Luxury / Premium** | Brand Equity | Cinema + Glossy Magazines + Experiential | Selective (2–3) | Emotional, minimal |
| **Commercial Vehicle** | Fleet Conversion | B2B Trade Shows + LinkedIn + Trade Press | High (4–5) | Rational, ROI-focused |

## 3.5 Derivation: Optimal Media Mix via Marginal Analysis

The optimal media mix occurs when the **marginal reach per rupee** is equal across all media vehicles:

$$ \frac{\partial R_{TV}}{\partial C_{TV}} = \frac{\partial R_{YT}}{\partial C_{YT}} = \frac{\partial R_{Print}}{\partial C_{Print}} = \lambda $$

Where $\lambda$ is the **shadow price** of the budget constraint. This is the economic foundation of media planning optimization – widely used in agency-side media-buying algorithms.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1:** Allocate initial budget to the medium with the **highest reach-per-rupee** (e.g., YouTube)

**Step 2:** Diminishing returns set in (each subsequent exposure is to a less-responsive viewer)

**Step 3:** At the margin, switch to the next-best medium (e.g., TV news)

**Step 4:** Continue until budget is exhausted and marginal returns are equalized

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Short-Answer Trick:** When asked "How do you decide the media mix?" – answer with **"Marginal analysis: equate the marginal reach per rupee across media."** This is a 3-marker winner.

## 3.6 Campaign Evaluation Decision Matrix

| Stage | Test Type | Key Metric | Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-launch** | Concept Test | Comprehension, Persuasion | Focus groups, surveys |
| **Pre-launch** | Copy Test | Recall, Attractiveness | Day-after recall test |
| **Mid-campaign** | Tracking | Ad recall, Message takeout | Aided/Unaided recall |
| **Post-campaign** | Sales Effect | Sales lift, Market share | Regression, Geo-test |
| **Post-campaign** | Communication | Attitude shift | Brand health tracker |
| **Long-term** | Brand Equity | Brand strength index | Interbrand, BrandZ |

$$ \text{Campaign Effectiveness} = w_1 \cdot \Delta \text{Recall} + w_2 \cdot \Delta \text{Attitude} + w_3 \cdot \Delta \text{Sales} $$

Where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are weights assigned to each dimension (typically 30-30-40).
