# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define "misleading advertisement" as per the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. What is the maximum penalty for a first-time offender?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
> **Definition (2 marks)**: Under **Section 2(47)** of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, a "misleading advertisement" refers to any advertisement that:
> - (a) falsely describes any product or service;
> - (b) gives a false guarantee or is likely to mislead consumers about the nature, substance, quantity, or quality of a product; or
> - (c) deliberately conceals important information.
>
> **Penalty (1 mark)**: For a **first violation**, the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) can impose a penalty of up to **₹10 lakh**. For a repeat violation, this increases to **₹50 lakh**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is "Greenwashing"? Provide one example from the automotive sector.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
> **Definition (2 marks)**: Greenwashing is the practice of making **unsubstantiated or misleading claims** about the environmental benefits of a product or service to attract eco-conscious consumers. It exploits the rising demand for sustainable products without delivering genuine environmental value.
>
> **Example (1 mark)**: A large SUV manufacturer markets its vehicle as *"Earth-Friendly"* because it uses *recycled plastic in the seat fabric*, while the 2.5-tonne vehicle still emits ~200 g CO₂/km and fails the CAFE norms. The single sustainable feature is exaggerated to mask a high-emission product.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme)**: Each Part B question offers an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. Both options below carry equal marks but test different cognitive levels.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**Case Scenario**: *Zenith Motors, a leading Indian car manufacturer, recently launched a TV commercial for its new hatchback "ZenDrive." The ad features a popular Bollywood star driving the car through mountainous terrain while a voiceover claims:*

> *"India's most fuel-efficient car — guaranteed 40 km/litre on Indian roads!"*

*The ad does not display any disclaimer. Two weeks later, a YouTube tech reviewer reveals that the actual certified mileage by ARAI is 24.5 km/l. The video goes viral with 5 million views. A consumer, Mr. Rajeev, files a complaint at the District Consumer Forum.*

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** *Identify the legal issues arising from Zenith Motors' advertisement. State the relevant sections of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 and the ASCI Code clauses that are violated. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss the remedies available to Mr. Rajeev and the regulatory actions the CCPA can take against Zenith Motors. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Issue 1: Misleading Advertisement under CPA, 2019 (2 marks)**
The ad's claim of *"guaranteed 40 km/l"* is a **false representation** because the ARAI-certified mileage is 24.5 km/l. This violates **Section 2(47)(a)** of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, which defines a misleading advertisement as one that *"falsely describes the product or service."* The **56% inflated claim** is material — it directly influences a rational consumer's purchase decision.

**Issue 2: Unfair Trade Practice under CPA, 2019 (1 mark)**
By making a false claim about the mileage, Zenith Motors is engaging in an *unfair trade practice* under **Section 2(28)(a)** of the Act, which prohibits *"making a false or misleading representation about the need or quality of any goods."*

**Issue 3: Violation of ASCI Code (2 marks)**
The ASCI Code (specifically the **Code on Disclaimers** and **Code on Automotive Advertising**) requires that:
- Performance claims must be based on **verifiable test data** (ARAI/ICAT certification).
- Superlative claims like *"India's most fuel-efficient"* require a **valid comparative ranking** from an independent body.
- Disclaimers must be **clearly visible and audible**, not buried in small print.

Zenith Motors violated **all three** ASCI requirements.

**Issue 4: Missing Disclaimer (1 mark)**
The ad fails to mention *"under standard test conditions"* or *"as certified by ARAI."* This omission violates the **ASCI Code on Disclaimers (2018)**.

**Issue 5: Use of Celebrity Endorser (1 mark)**
Under the **CCPA Guidelines on Celebrities (2023)**, a celebrity endorsing a misleading ad is also liable. The Bollywood star in the ZenDrive ad can be penalized up to ₹10 lakh and may face a ban on future endorsements for up to 1 year.

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Remedies Available to Mr. Rajeev (3 marks)**
Under **Section 35 of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019**, Mr. Rajeev can file a complaint at the **District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission** (assuming the car's value is below ₹1 Crore). He can seek the following reliefs:
1. **Replacement** of the vehicle with a model that delivers the promised mileage, or
2. **Refund** of the purchase price with interest (typically 6–9% per annum), and
3. **Compensation for mental agony and harassment** (typically ₹5,000–₹1 lakh depending on the Commission's discretion), and
4. **Cost of litigation**.

**Regulatory Actions by CCPA (3 marks)**
Under **Sections 89–94 of the CPA, 2019**, the CCPA can:
1. Issue a **cease-and-desist order** directing Zenith Motors to immediately stop airing the ad.
2. Impose a **penalty of up to ₹10 lakh** for the first violation.
3. Order a **mandatory recall** of the ZenDrive vehicles under Section 94, at Zenith's cost.
4. File a **class action** if the violation affects a large group of consumers (Section 73).
5. Refer the matter for **criminal prosecution** if the violation persists.

**Ethical Dimension (1 mark)**
Beyond legal penalties, Zenith Motors has suffered significant **reputational damage**. A 2023 ASCI study found that **78% of consumers** will boycott a brand involved in misleading advertising. The ethical lesson: *transparency in marketing is not just a legal obligation but a commercial imperative*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Module 4 Question]**

**(a)** *Explain the three-tier consumer grievance redressal machinery under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. Discuss the landmark Maruti Udyog Ltd. v. Susheel Kumar Goyal (2007) judgment and its relevance. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss the environmental responsibilities of automotive marketers in India. Cover BS-VI norms, CAFE norms, and the concept of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**The Three-Tier Machinery (5 marks)**
The Consumer Protection Act, 2019 establishes a **hierarchical three-tier** redressal system to ensure that consumers can access justice proportionate to the value of their grievance:

| Tier | Forum | Pecuniary Jurisdiction | Composition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **District** | District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission | Up to **₹1 Crore** | President + 2 members |
| **State** | State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission | **₹1 Crore to ₹10 Crore** | President + 4 members |
| **National** | National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC), New Delhi | Above **₹10 Crore** | President + 6 members |

**Key Procedural Features**:
- **No court fee** for claims up to ₹5 lakh (in district forum).
- **Time-bound disposal**: 90 days from filing (extendable to 150 days for complex cases).
- **Right to Appeal**: A party can appeal to the next higher forum within **30 days** of the order.
- **Article 136**: Final appeal lies with the **Supreme Court of India**.

**Landmark Case: Maruti Udyog Ltd. v. Susheel Kumar Goyal (2007) (2 marks)**
The Supreme Court, in this case, held that:
- A **corporate entity** that purchases goods **for commercial use** (e.g., a company buying 50 cars for its employees) is a "consumer" under the Act.
- However, a person purchasing goods for **self-employment or earning a livelihood** (e.g., a taxi driver buying a car) is **not** a consumer.
- **Relevance to Automotive Marketing**: This distinction is crucial for B2B sales, fleet management, and dealer-corporate transactions. It defines who can invoke the consumer forum's jurisdiction.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**1. BS-VI Emission Norms (2 marks)**
- Effective from **April 1, 2020**, BS-VI norms are equivalent to **Euro 6** standards.
- India **skipped BS-V entirely** — a major leapfrog.
- Key reductions: **80% drop in particulate matter (PM)**, **25% drop in NOx** for diesel vehicles.
- Marketers must disclose the BS-VI compliance of every vehicle in advertising and brochures.

**2. CAFE Norms (2 marks)**
- **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE) Norms** were introduced in **2015** and tightened in **2022**.
- **Phase 2 Target (2022–2027)**: **113.5 g CO₂/km** fleet average, equivalent to ~**20 km/l** for petrol cars.
- **Penalties** for non-compliance:
  - ₹25,000 per car for shortfall of ≤ 0.2 g/km.
  - Scaling up to ₹5 crore for severe violations.
- **Marketing Implication**: Manufacturers must push EVs and hybrids to balance their CAFE portfolio, leading to more EV-centric marketing.

**3. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) under ELV Rules (2 marks)**
- The **End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Rules** mandate that **manufacturers are responsible** for the entire lifecycle of a vehicle.
- Targets: **85% recyclability** and **95% recoverability** by weight.
- Manufacturers must set up **collection centres**, finance recycling, and ensure safe disposal of **batteries, oils, and tyres**.
- **Marketing Implication**: Auto marketers increasingly highlight the *recyclability* and *sustainability* of their vehicles as a brand differentiator.

**4. Ethical Reflection (1 mark)**
Environmental marketing regulations push the industry from a *linear economy* (produce-use-discard) to a *circular economy* (produce-use-recycle). Marketers who genuinely embrace sustainability will gain **long-term customer trust** and **regulatory goodwill** — a competitive advantage that transcends short-term sales.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic (Repeated KTU Examiner Observations)**
>
> 1. **Confusing ASCI with CCPA**: ASCI is a *self-regulatory body* (no statutory power); CCPA is a *statutory authority* with penal power. Many students wrongly attribute ASCI's "ruling" with legal enforceability.
> 2. **Citing the 1986 Act**: The Consumer Protection Act, **2019** replaced the 1986 Act. Students who cite the old Act lose easy marks.
> 3. **Skipping the Section Number**: Examiners award **at least 1 mark per question** for quoting the correct *Section 2(47)*, *Section 2(28)*, *Section 89*, etc. Vague references like "as per the Consumer Act" will be marked down.
> 4. **Ignoring Ethical Dimension**: The 2024 Scheme's OBE pattern tests **Level 5 (Evaluate)**. An answer that only lists laws without reflecting on *ethical implications* is incomplete and typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing BS-VI and BS-IV Limits**: Confusing the g/km thresholds between BS-IV and BS-VI is a common error. Memorize the table in Section 2.3.1.
> 6. **Forgetting the ASCI Year**: Established in **1985**. Often confused with 1995 or 2005.
> 7. **Omitting the Four-Word Disclaimer**: A mileage claim without *"under standard test conditions"* is automatically a violation. Students often miss this nuance.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Legal & Ethical Issues in Automotive Marketing**
>
> - **Consumer Protection Act, 2019** is the *primary statute* — not the 1986 Act.
> - **Three pillars**: Advertising Regulations, Consumer Protection, Environmental Responsibility.
> - **ASCI** is *self-regulatory* (1985); **CCPA** is *statutory* (under CPA 2019).
> - **CCPA Penalty**: ₹10 lakh (1st), ₹50 lakh (2nd), ₹10 crore (child-targeted).
> - **Section 2(47)** = definition of misleading advertisement.
> - **Section 2(28)** = definition of unfair trade practice.
> - **Section 2(34)** = definition of product liability.
> - **Sections 89–94** = CCPA powers (penalty, recall, cease & desist).
> - **Three-tier redressal**: District (≤₹1 Cr), State (₹1–10 Cr), National (>₹10 Cr).
> - **Maruti Udyog v. Susheel Kumar Goyal (2007)**: corporate buyers are consumers; self-employed buyers are not.
> - **BS-VI** effective **April 1, 2020**; **PM reduced by 80%**, **NOx by 25%** (diesel).
> - **CAFE Phase 2 target**: **113.5 g CO₂/km**.
> - **ELV Rules**: 85% recyclable, 95% recoverable.
> - **Greenwashing** = misleading eco-claims.
> - **EPCA** = Environment Pollution (Prevention and Control) Authority, under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.
> - **DPDP Act, 2023** = governs connected car data.
> - **Competition Act, 2002** governs predatory pricing and anti-competitive practices.
> - **Kantian ethics**: treat the consumer as an end, not a means.
> - **Cost of compliance << Cost of non-compliance** (use the E(Cost) formula in 14-mark answers).
> - **Always quote the Section number** — this is the single biggest differentiator between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.
